content_rubrics.title: ETI increases sputum microbiome α-diversity over time
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy leads to an increase in sputum microbiome α-diversity at measured time points (e.g., 1, 3, and 12 months) compared to pre-treatment baseline.

content_rubrics.title: ETI reduces relative abundance of Pseudomonas aeruginosa
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that ETI therapy decreases the relative abundance of the pathogen Pseudomonas aeruginosa in sputum, with a notable reduction observed by approximately 3 months after treatment initiation.

content_rubrics.title: Modulator therapy does not normalize microbiome to healthy levels
content_rubrics.description: The model should convey that while CFTR modulators like ETI induce positive changes, the airway microbiome measures (e.g., diversity, pathogen load) do not return to levels seen in healthy individuals, indicating a partial rather than complete restoration.

content_rubrics.title: ETI shifts sputum proteome and reduces inflammation markers
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that alongside microbiome changes, ETI therapy shifts the sputum proteome towards a healthier profile and reduces specific markers of inflammation, such as interleukin-8 and free neutrophil elastase activity.

content_rubrics.title: Microbiome changes involve community restructuring
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that the effect of CFTR modulators on the airway microbiome involves a restructuring of the microbial community, such as changes in the balance between pathogens and anaerobes and increased evenness, while often retaining a person-specific signature.

content_rubrics.title: High-frequency sampling reveals increased microbiome dynamics on ETI
content_rubrics.description: The model should indicate that studies using high-frequency longitudinal sampling suggest the airway ecosystem becomes more dynamic following ETI initiation, even though chronic infection with CF pathogens often persists.

question: Across clinical studies of Alzheimer’s disease, how do microglial activation markers measured by PET imaging relate to cognitive trajectory and neurodegeneration biomarkers?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: TSPO as the primary PET target for microglial activation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the 18 kDa translocator protein (TSPO) is the primary index of neuroinflammation and the dominant target for PET radiotracers used to measure microglial activation in Alzheimer's disease studies.

content_rubrics.title: Cross-sectional link: higher TSPO signal with worse cognition
content_rubrics.description: The answer must mention that cross-sectional studies in amyloid-positive MCI/AD cohorts have found an inverse correlation, where higher TSPO PET binding (e.g., with tracers like [^11C]PBR28 or [^11C]ER176) is associated with worse cognitive performance and greater clinical severity.

content_rubrics.title: Specific finding in early-onset MCI due to AD
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the specific finding from the retrieved paper (PMID: 39013020) that in early-onset amnestic MCI due to Alzheimer's disease, TSPO PET signal (using [^11C]ER176) in AD-vulnerable regions correlates with worse cognitive scores.

content_rubrics.title: Heterogeneous longitudinal findings on cognitive trajectory
content_rubrics.description: The answer must describe the mixed or heterogeneous findings when TSPO PET is used to predict longitudinal cognitive decline, noting that some studies show higher baseline binding predicting slower decline, while increasing signal over time correlates with worse outcomes.

content_rubrics.title: Co-localization of inflammation with tau pathology
content_rubrics.description: Based on the retrieved paper (PMID: 39013020), the answer should mention that brain inflammation measured by TSPO PET (specifically with [^11C]ER176) highly co-localizes with tau pathology in patients with mild cognitive impairment due to early-onset Alzheimer's disease.

content_rubrics.title: Tracer limitations and the role of [^11C]ER176
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note a key limitation of many TSPO PET tracers: their inability to image subjects with a low-binder TSPO rs6971 genotype. It should also mention that novel tracers like [^11C]ER176 address this by allowing imaging in participants with any TSPO genotype.

content_rubrics.title: S1320 trial primary PFS outcome for intermittent dosing
content_rubrics.description: The model must state that in the S1320 randomized phase 2 trial (NCT02196181), continuous dosing of dabrafenib+trametinib yielded a statistically significant improvement in post-randomization progression-free survival (median 9.0 months) compared with intermittent dosing (median 5.5 months), contrary to the initial hypothesis.

content_rubrics.title: INTERIM trial primary endpoint outcome for intermittent dosing
content_rubrics.description: The model must state that in the INTERIM randomized phase 2 trial, the intermittent dosing arm (dabrafenib d1-21, trametinib d1-14 every 28 days) was inferior to continuous dosing for the composite primary endpoint (including progression-free survival and quality of life).

content_rubrics.title: Lack of overall survival benefit in S1320 trial
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the S1320 trial found no differences in the secondary outcome of overall survival between the continuous and intermittent dosing groups.

content_rubrics.title: Lack of overall toxicity reduction in S1320 trial
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the S1320 trial reported no differences in the overall incidence of treatment-associated toxicity between the continuous and intermittent dosing groups.

content_rubrics.title: Preclinical rationale for intermittent dosing
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite the preclinical rationale mentioned in the S1320 trial abstract: that intermittent BRAF inhibitor therapy may delay acquired resistance based on preclinical modeling.

content_rubrics.title: INTERIM trial patient population characteristics
content_rubrics.description: The model can mention that the INTERIM trial recruited patients with BRAF V600 mutant advanced melanoma, including a population with 29% having brain metastases and 65% AJCC (7th ed) stage IV M1c.

content_rubrics.title: NLRP3/IL-1β role in TET2 clonal hematopoiesis heart failure
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that in murine heart failure models, Tet2 deficiency in hematopoietic or myeloid cells worsens cardiac remodeling and function, and this is associated with increased interleukin-1beta (IL-1β) expression. This demonstrates a mechanistic link between clonal hematopoiesis and adverse cardiac remodeling via the IL-1β/NLRP3 pathway.

content_rubrics.title: NLRP3 inhibition reverses TET2-deficiency effects
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that treatment with a selective NLRP3 inflammasome inhibitor protected against heart failure development in Tet2-deficient mice and eliminated the differences in cardiac parameters between Tet2-deficient and wild-type mice, indicating the pathway's causal role.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical implication of TET2 mutations for therapy
content_rubrics.description: Based on the translational study, the answer should note the conclusion that individuals with TET2-mediated clonal hematopoiesis may be at greater risk for heart failure and might respond better to therapies targeting IL-1β-NLRP3 inflammasome inhibition.

content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the specific primary kidney composite outcome comparison between finerenone and placebo groups in FIDELIO-DKD trial: 17.8% vs 21.1% with HR 0.82, resulting in 3.3% absolute risk reduction over 2.6 years.

content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the incidence rates of hyperkalemia with placebo treatment: 9.2% for ≥mild hyperkalemia and 1.4% for moderate hyperkalemia over 2.6 years median follow-up in FIDELIO-DKD trial.

content_rubrics.description: The model should mention key independent risk factors for hyperkalemia identified in the analysis: higher serum potassium, lower eGFR, increased urine albumin-creatinine ratio, younger age, female sex, β-blocker use, and finerenone assignment.

content_rubrics.title: Hyperkalemia management strategy effectiveness
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that routine potassium monitoring and hyperkalemia management strategies minimized the impact of hyperkalemia, providing basis for clinical use of finerenone.

question: In coronary artery disease, how do elevated lipoprotein(a) levels contribute to atherosclerotic risk through pro-inflammatory or pro-thrombotic mechanisms, and how consistent is the association across cohorts?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Lp(a) as a major carrier of oxidized phospholipids (OxPL)
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that lipoprotein(a) is a significant carrier of oxidized phospholipids among apoB-containing particles, which is a key pro-inflammatory mechanism linking Lp(a) to vascular inflammation and plaque activity.

content_rubrics.title: Structural homology of apo(a) to plasminogen and antifibrinolytic effects
content_rubrics.description: The model should explain that the apolipoprotein(a) component of Lp(a) is structurally similar to plasminogen and can compete for binding sites, thereby inhibiting fibrinolysis and contributing to a pro-thrombotic state.

content_rubrics.title: Association of Lp(a) and OxPL biomarkers with CAD severity and outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite specific evidence (e.g., from the CASABLANCA study) showing that Lp(a) and OxPL biomarkers are strongly correlated and that each is independently associated with multivessel coronary artery disease and major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE).

content_rubrics.title: Interaction between high Lp(a) and inflammatory markers for amplified CHD risk
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention findings (e.g., from the Nurses' Health Study) indicating that the coronary heart disease risk associated with elevated Lp(a) is greater when inflammatory or hemostatic markers like C-reactive protein (CRP) or fibrinogen are also high.

content_rubrics.title: Lp(a) increase via IL-6 response element during inflammation
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that the LPA gene contains an interleukin-6 (IL-6) response element, which can lead to an acute-phase increase in Lp(a) levels following a cytokine storm, as hypothesized in conditions like COVID-19, potentially amplifying risk.

content_rubrics.title: Lp(a) contribution to plaque destabilization and acute events
content_rubrics.description: The model should describe how elevated Lp(a), through its pro-inflammatory and pro-thrombotic properties, may lead to the destabilization of pre-existing atherosclerotic plaques, potentially triggering acute myocardial infarction or stroke.

question: In advanced non-small cell lung cancer, what is the comparative efficacy of anti-PD-1 versus anti-PD-L1 monotherapy when stratified by tumor PD-L1 expression levels?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

content_rubrics.title: No direct head-to-head phase III trials
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explicitly state that there are no phase III randomized controlled trials directly comparing anti-PD-1 monotherapy versus anti-PD-L1 monotherapy in advanced NSCLC, making indirect comparisons the primary evidence source.

content_rubrics.title: Anti-PD-1 superior to anti-PD-L1 for OS
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the network meta-analysis finding that, overall, anti-PD-1 monotherapy provides superior overall survival (OS) compared to anti-PD-L1 monotherapy in advanced NSCLC patients.

content_rubrics.title: Superior OS of anti-PD-1 regimens in PD-L1 ≥1%
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the specific finding that for patients with PD-L1 expression ≥1%, treatment regimens containing anti-PD-1 provided superior OS benefits compared to those containing anti-PD-L1.

content_rubrics.title: Optimal regimens differ by PD-L1 expression level
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the network meta-analysis identified different optimal treatments for OS and PFS depending on specific PD-L1 expression subgroups (e.g., <1%, 1-49%, ≥50%).

content_rubrics.title: Cemiplimab ranked best for OS without PD-L1 selection

content_rubrics.title: Oral iron does not improve peak VO₂ in HFrEF with iron deficiency
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that in the IRONOUT HF trial, high-dose oral iron polysaccharide (150 mg twice daily for 16 weeks) did not significantly improve peak oxygen uptake (peak VO₂) compared to placebo in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and iron deficiency.

content_rubrics.title: Oral iron does not improve 6-minute walk distance in HFrEF
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that in the IRONOUT HF trial, oral iron supplementation did not result in a significant improvement in the 6-minute walk distance (6MWD) compared to placebo over 16 weeks.

content_rubrics.title: IV iron improves peak VO₂ compared to oral iron in anemic HF patients
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that in the IRON-HF trial, intravenous (IV) iron sucrose significantly increased peak VO₂, while oral ferrous sulfate did not, indicating superior functional capacity improvement with IV therapy in anemic heart failure patients with low transferrin saturation.

content_rubrics.title: IV iron reduces risk of hospitalization for worsening HF
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that in the CONFIRM-HF trial, intravenous ferric carboxymaltose was associated with a significantly lower risk of hospitalization for worsening heart failure, with a reported hazard ratio of 0.39 (95% CI 0.19–0.82) compared to placebo.

content_rubrics.title: Lack of evidence for oral iron reducing HF hospitalizations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that robust trials of oral iron in HFrEF with iron deficiency (specifically referencing IRONOUT HF) did not show clinical benefit for exercise capacity, and corresponding evidence that oral iron reduces heart failure hospitalizations is lacking.

question: In rheumatoid arthritis with inadequate response to methotrexate, how do rates of major adverse cardiovascular events and venous thromboembolism compare between JAK inhibitors and TNF inhibitors in randomized and post-marketing comparative studies?
topic: Immunology / Safety signal characterization across development stages

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Study design and population characteristics
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the comparison comes from a phase 3b/4 randomized safety study (ORAL Surveillance) in rheumatoid arthritis patients aged ≥50 years with ≥1 additional cardiovascular risk factor, on background methotrexate.

content_rubrics.title: Extended MACE composite definitions
content_rubrics.description: The model should describe how MACE endpoints were extended sequentially from MACE-3 (non-fatal myocardial infarction, non-fatal stroke, or cardiovascular death) to MACE-8 (adding hospitalization for unstable angina, coronary revascularisation, transient ischemic attack, peripheral vascular disease, and heart failure hospitalization).

content_rubrics.title: MACE-4 to MACE-8 hazard ratio comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that hazard ratios for MACE-4 through MACE-8 were similar between tofacitinib (both 5 mg and 10 mg doses) and TNF inhibitors, indicating no clear separation in risk for composites limited to ischemic CV events and heart failure hospitalization.

content_rubrics.title: VTE incorporation into composite endpoint
content_rubrics.description: The model should explain that venous thromboembolism (VTE) was added to create the MACE-8 plus VTE composite endpoint, and that risk for this combined endpoint showed dose-dependent differences between tofacitinib and TNF inhibitors.

content_rubrics.title: Specific hazard ratios for MACE-8 plus VTE
content_rubrics.description: The model should provide the specific hazard ratios: tofacitinib 5 mg BID vs TNF inhibitors showed HR 1.12 (0.82 to 1.52), while tofacitinib 10 mg BID vs TNF inhibitors showed HR 1.38 (1.02 to 1.85).

content_rubrics.title: Driving factor for increased risk
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that the totality of cardiovascular risk was higher with tofacitinib 10 mg BID versus TNF inhibitors, driven primarily by an increase in venous thromboembolism (VTE) events.

content_rubrics.title: Individual myocardial infarction risk
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that risk of myocardial infarction (MI) was higher with tofacitinib versus TNF inhibitors, while differences in risk of other individual cardiovascular events were not clearly suggested.

content_rubrics.title: Risk stratification by patient characteristics
content_rubrics.description: The model should indicate that across extended MACE definitions, risk appeared higher with tofacitinib versus TNF inhibitors in patients with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease or age ≥65 years.

question: In nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), how do GLP-1 receptor agonists compare with pioglitazone in histologic resolution of steatohepatitis and improvement in fibrosis stage?

content_rubrics.title: GLP-1RA Efficacy for NASH Resolution
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that GLP-1 receptor agonists significantly improve histologic resolution of NASH without worsening of liver fibrosis, citing the meta-analysis finding (WMD: 4.08, 95%CI 2.54-6.56, p<0.00001) from the systematic review (PMID: 38555202).

content_rubrics.title: Indirect Comparison Context
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explicitly state that direct head-to-head comparisons between GLP-1RAs and pioglitazone are lacking, and conclusions are drawn from indirect comparisons across separate trials with differing populations, durations, and endpoints.

content_rubrics.title: Pioglitazone's Histologic Improvement
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the PIVENS trial finding that pioglitazone led to 'improvement in NASH' in 34% of nondiabetic patients vs. 19% with placebo, while noting its primary endpoint did not meet the prespecified significance threshold (PMID: 20427778).

content_rubrics.title: Comparative Efficacy Ranking
content_rubrics.description: The answer should incorporate findings from the network meta-analysis (PMID: 39903735) that compares the relative rank order of pharmacological agents, including GLP-1RAs and pioglitazone, for fibrosis regression and MASH resolution.

content_rubrics.title: Fibrosis Improvement with Pioglitazone
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that in the PIVENS trial, pioglitazone did not show a statistically significant improvement in fibrosis stage compared to placebo (PMID: 20427778).

content_rubrics.title: Evidence Source for GLP-1RAs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that evidence for GLP-1RAs (liraglutide, semaglutide) comes from specific trials like LEAN and phase 2/3 semaglutide studies, which are included in the systematic review and meta-analysis (PMID: 38555202).

content_rubrics.title: Overall survival comparison from CheckMate 067 6.5-year update
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the specific median overall survival (OS) figures from the CheckMate 067 trial with minimum 6.5-year follow-up: 72.1 months for nivolumab+ipilimumab combination, 36.9 months for nivolumab monotherapy, and 19.9 months for ipilimumab monotherapy.

content_rubrics.title: Melanoma-specific survival (MSS) outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The model should include the descriptive analysis of melanoma-specific survival (MSS) from CheckMate 067: median MSS was not reached for the combination, 58.7 months for nivolumab alone, and 21.9 months for ipilimumab alone.

content_rubrics.title: Long-term survival rates by BRAF mutation status
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the 6.5-year overall survival rates stratified by BRAF mutation status from CheckMate 067: for BRAF-mutant patients, rates were 57% (combination), 43% (nivolumab), 25% (ipilimumab); for BRAF-wild-type, rates were 46%, 42%, and 22%, respectively.

content_rubrics.title: Treatment-free interval after discontinuation
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the median treatment-free interval for patients who discontinued therapy in CheckMate 067: 27.6 months for the combination group, 2.3 months for nivolumab monotherapy, and 1.9 months for ipilimumab monotherapy.

content_rubrics.title: Safety profile and new safety signals
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that according to the 6.5-year CheckMate 067 update, no new safety signals were observed since the 5-year analysis, indicating a well-characterized long-term adverse event profile for both regimens.

question: In cystic fibrosis with chronic Pseudomonas infection, how does inhaled tobramycin compare with inhaled aztreonam in reducing exacerbation frequency and improving quality of life?

content_rubrics.title: Evidence source for comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the evidence comes from a randomized controlled trial (RCT) comparing inhaled aztreonam lysine (AZLI) versus inhaled tobramycin nebulizer solution (TNS) over three 28-day on/off cycles, as reported in the retrieved paper (pmid: 22985692).

content_rubrics.title: Exacerbation reduction with AZLI
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that inhaled aztreonam (AZLI) resulted in fewer respiratory hospitalizations (p=0.044) compared to inhaled tobramycin (TNS) in the comparator trial.

content_rubrics.title: Additional antibiotic events reduction
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that AZLI led to fewer respiratory events requiring additional antipseudomonal antibiotics (p=0.004) compared to TNS, indicating reduced exacerbation burden.

content_rubrics.title: Quality of life evidence limitation
content_rubrics.description: The model should acknowledge that while AZLI showed improvement in CFQ-R respiratory symptom scores in placebo-controlled trials, direct head-to-head quality of life comparison between AZLI and TNS may not be explicitly reported in the retrieved evidence.

content_rubrics.title: TNS efficacy for eradication
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that TNS versus placebo may improve Pseudomonas aeruginosa eradication (odds ratio 0.06 at one month, 0.15 at two months), based on evidence from multiple trials in the retrieved paper (pmid: 37268599).

content_rubrics.title: Treatment duration comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that one trial comparing 28-day versus 56-day TNS treatment found little difference in time to next isolation (hazard ratio 0.8), suggesting treatment duration may not significantly affect outcomes.

content_rubrics.title: Study population characteristics
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that the evidence comes from trials involving people with cystic fibrosis (CF) with recent Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolation from respiratory secretions, as per the inclusion criteria of the retrieved papers.

content_rubrics.title: BRCA1/2 mutation association with platinum sensitivity
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that BRCA1/2 loss-of-function mutations (germline or somatic) are associated with a higher likelihood of platinum sensitivity and lower likelihood of platinum-resistant/refractory relapse, based on the finding that platinum-refractory/resistant recurrences occurred essentially only in tumors without BRCA loss-of-function.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical evidence for BRCA-mutant platinum sensitivity
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite clinical evidence that BRCA-mutant ovarian cancers are especially platinum sensitive and are associated with improved outcomes compared to BRCA-proficient disease, as summarized in the review paper.

content_rubrics.title: SOLO1 trial: Long-term OS benefit in BRCA-mutated patients
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that maintenance olaparib provided a sustained progression-free and overall survival benefit in patients with newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer and a BRCA1/2 mutation who responded to platinum-based chemotherapy, supporting the underlying platinum sensitivity in this population.

content_rubrics.title: Niraparib efficacy in platinum-sensitive recurrent disease
content_rubrics.description: The model should reference that niraparib maintenance therapy showed efficacy in patients with platinum-sensitive, recurrent ovarian cancer, with patients categorized by germline BRCA mutation status, implying a link between HR status and the platinum-sensitive state.

content_rubrics.title: HRD beyond BRCA and platinum response correlation
content_rubrics.description: The model should discuss that homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) as a broad category often correlates with platinum sensitivity, but the strength of prediction can vary depending on the specific cause of the HRD.

all_rubrics.title: BRCA1/2 mutation association with platinum sensitivity
all_rubrics.description: The model should state that BRCA1/2 loss-of-function mutations (germline or somatic) are associated with a higher likelihood of platinum sensitivity and lower likelihood of platinum-resistant/refractory relapse, based on the finding that platinum-refractory/resistant recurrences occurred essentially only in tumors without BRCA loss-of-function.

all_rubrics.title: Clinical evidence for BRCA-mutant platinum sensitivity
all_rubrics.description: The model should cite clinical evidence that BRCA-mutant ovarian cancers are especially platinum sensitive and are associated with improved outcomes compared to BRCA-proficient disease, as summarized in the review paper.

all_rubrics.title: SOLO1 trial: Long-term OS benefit in BRCA-mutated patients
all_rubrics.description: The model should mention that maintenance olaparib provided a sustained progression-free and overall survival benefit in patients with newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer and a BRCA1/2 mutation who responded to platinum-based chemotherapy, supporting the underlying platinum sensitivity in this population.

all_rubrics.title: Niraparib efficacy in platinum-sensitive recurrent disease
all_rubrics.description: The model should reference that niraparib maintenance therapy showed efficacy in patients with platinum-sensitive, recurrent ovarian cancer, with patients categorized by germline BRCA mutation status, implying a link between HR status and the platinum-sensitive state.

all_rubrics.title: HRD beyond BRCA and platinum response correlation
all_rubrics.description: The model should discuss that homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) as a broad category often correlates with platinum sensitivity, but the strength of prediction can vary depending on the specific cause of the HRD.

question: What is the association between specific CFTR genotypes and risk of cystic fibrosis–related diabetes, and how does this impact clinical outcomes?

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: CF carriers have increased risk for diabetes
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that cystic fibrosis carriers (individuals with CF mutations) are at increased risk for diabetes, as shown in the population-based study where diabetes was among the CF-related conditions more prevalent among carriers compared to controls.

content_rubrics.title: All CF-related conditions more prevalent in carriers
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that all 59 CF-related diagnostic conditions studied were more prevalent among CF carriers compared to controls, with significantly increased risk for 57 of these conditions, indicating a broad risk elevation pattern.

content_rubrics.title: Population-level morbidity substantial due to carrier numbers
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that while individual-level risk for most conditions remains low, population-level morbidity attributable to the CF carrier state is substantial because there are more than 10 million carriers in the US.

content_rubrics.title: Genetic testing informs prevention and treatment
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that genetic testing may inform prevention, diagnosis, and treatment for a broad range of CF carrier-related conditions, including diabetes.

content_rubrics.title: CFTR dysfunction causes pancreatic insufficiency
content_rubrics.description: The model should describe that CFTR dysfunction results in pancreatic insufficiency as one of the main clinical features of cystic fibrosis disease.

content_rubrics.title: CFTR modulators restore ion transport
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention CFTR modulators as oral medications that partially restore ion transport, with around 80% of people with CF being genetically eligible for them in Europe.

question: In patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors, what is the effect of baseline systemic corticosteroid use on overall survival and response rates, based on clinical trial subgroup analyses and real-world cohorts?
topic: Oncology / Concomitant medication impact on trial and real-world outcomes

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: RADIOHEAD cohort baseline steroid use and OS
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that in the RADIOHEAD prospective real-world cohort, patients receiving corticosteroids prior to ICI initiation had significantly worse survival outcomes (HR 1.37, p=0.0054), with a stronger association for systemic steroid use (HR 1.75, p=0.0022).

content_rubrics.title: RADIOHEAD confirms baseline steroid link to worse response
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that the RADIOHEAD study conclusion explicitly identifies baseline steroid use as being associated with a worse response to ICI treatment after adjustment for survival bias.

content_rubrics.title: Atezo-Brain trial baseline steroid prevalence
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite the specific finding from the Atezo-Brain phase II trial in NSCLC with brain metastases that 55% of patients were on baseline corticosteroids (dexamethasone up to 4 mg/day).

content_rubrics.title: Atezo-Brain trial lacks steroid-stratified efficacy
content_rubrics.description: The model should note the limitation that, despite high prevalence, the cited abstract for the Atezo-Brain trial does not report outcomes (OS/ORR) stratified by baseline steroid use.

content_rubrics.title: RADIOHEAD demonstrates irAE association with improved OS
content_rubrics.description: The model should reference the RADIOHEAD finding that patients experiencing any irAE had significantly improved real-world overall survival (HR=0.41, 95% CI=(0.31, 0.55)), providing context for the steroid analysis within the same cohort.

content_rubrics.title: Real-world evidence from RADIOHEAD cohort
content_rubrics.description: The model should identify the RADIOHEAD study as a source of large, prospective, real-world evidence (n=1,070) on the topic of baseline steroids and ICI outcomes.

question: In patients with atrial fibrillation and stable coronary artery disease beyond 12 months after PCI, does oral anticoagulant monotherapy compared with oral anticoagulant plus antiplatelet therapy reduce bleeding without increasing ischemic events?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: AFIRE trial primary efficacy outcome result
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that in the AFIRE trial, rivaroxaban monotherapy was noninferior to combination therapy for the composite efficacy endpoint (stroke, systemic embolism, MI, unstable angina requiring revascularization, or death), with a reported hazard ratio (HR) of 0.72 (95% CI 0.55–0.95).

content_rubrics.title: AFIRE trial primary safety outcome result
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that in the AFIRE trial, rivaroxaban monotherapy was superior to combination therapy for major bleeding (ISTH criteria), with a reported hazard ratio (HR) of 0.59 (95% CI 0.39–0.89).

content_rubrics.title: AFIRE stent subgroup efficacy result
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the specific hazard ratio for the efficacy composite endpoint in the AFIRE trial subgroup of patients with prior coronary stenting >1 year, which was HR 0.70 (95% CI: 0.50-0.98; P = 0.036).

content_rubrics.title: AFIRE stent subgroup ischemic endpoint result
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the specific result for ischemic endpoints (separate from the composite efficacy endpoint) in the AFIRE stent subgroup, which was a hazard ratio of 0.82 (95% CI: 0.58-1.15; P = 0.240), indicating no statistically significant increase.

content_rubrics.title: AFIRE stent subgroup safety result
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the specific hazard ratio for major bleeding in the AFIRE trial subgroup of patients with prior coronary stenting >1 year, which was HR 0.55 (95% CI: 0.33-0.92; P = 0.019).

content_rubrics.title: AFIRE trial population and design
content_rubrics.description: The model should describe the AFIRE trial population as patients with atrial fibrillation and stable coronary artery disease (PCI/CABG >1 year prior or angiographically confirmed CAD not requiring revascularization), and note it was an open-label, randomized trial.

content_rubrics.title: Trial termination context for AFIRE
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the AFIRE trial was stopped early due to higher mortality observed in the combination therapy group.

content_rubrics.title: OAC-ALONE trial conclusion
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that the OAC-ALONE trial, which compared OAC alone vs OAC plus SAPT in AF >1 year after stenting, was underpowered and produced inconclusive results due to early termination.

question: In adults with prediabetes, how effective are structured lifestyle interventions compared with metformin in preventing progression to type 2 diabetes over at least 2 years of follow-up?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: DPP 3-year results: comparative risk reduction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that in the Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) over ~2.8 years, structured lifestyle intervention reduced diabetes incidence by 58% versus placebo, while metformin reduced it by 31%, making lifestyle significantly more effective than metformin during this initial period.

content_rubrics.title: DPP 3-year incidence rates per 100 person-years
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the specific diabetes incidence rates from the DPP trial: 4.8 cases (lifestyle), 7.8 cases (metformin), and 11.0 cases (placebo) per 100 person-years, demonstrating the superior efficacy of lifestyle.

content_rubrics.title: Long-term (15-year) DPPOS hazard ratios
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the long-term (15-year) hazard ratios from the DPP Outcomes Study (DPPOS): a 27% reduction (HR 0.73) for the lifestyle group and an 18% reduction (HR 0.82) for the metformin group compared to placebo, showing persistent but attenuated effects.

content_rubrics.title: Cumulative incidence at 15 years in DPPOS
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the cumulative incidences of diabetes at year 15 in the DPPOS: 55% (lifestyle), 56% (metformin), and 62% (placebo), indicating a narrowing of the difference between lifestyle and metformin over time.

content_rubrics.title: Declining between-group differences over long-term follow-up
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the DPPOS showed declining between-group differences in diabetes prevention efficacy between the lifestyle and metformin groups over the long-term (15-year) follow-up.

content_rubrics.title: Microvascular outcomes in DPPOS by treatment group
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note the DPPOS finding that the overall prevalence of the aggregate microvascular outcome (nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy) was not significantly different between the lifestyle, metformin, and placebo groups in the total cohort.

content_rubrics.title: Microvascular outcomes in women from DPPOS
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify the DPPOS finding that, in women, the lifestyle intervention was associated with a significantly lower prevalence of microvascular complications compared to both placebo and metformin groups.

content_rubrics.title: Benefit of not progressing to diabetes on microvascular outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the DPPOS finding that participants who did not develop diabetes had a 28% lower prevalence of microvascular complications compared to those who did develop diabetes.

question: In solid tumors treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors, how does antibiotic exposure near treatment initiation affect objective response rate and overall survival?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Antibiotics disrupt gut microbiota affecting cancer immunity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that antibiotics can negatively impact the gut microbiome, which plays a crucial role in regulating systemic immune responses and shaping anti-tumor immunity, thereby potentially impairing immunotherapy efficacy.

content_rubrics.title: Gut microbiome signature predicts immunotherapy response
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the composition and diversity of the gut microbiota can serve as a biomarker, predicting clinical outcomes and the likelihood of response to immune checkpoint blockade therapy.

content_rubrics.title: Immune checkpoint inhibitors cause immune-related adverse events
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that ICIs (anti-CTLA-4, anti-PD-1) lead to a spectrum of immune-related adverse events (irAEs), which are distinct from the effects of antibiotics and require separate management.

content_rubrics.title: Antibiotics are a known cause of drug-induced interstitial lung disease
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that antibiotics themselves are among the drugs that can cause interstitial lung disease (DI-ILD), which is a separate and important consideration for patient morbidity and mortality, distinct from ICI efficacy.

content_rubrics.title: The gut microbiome-immune system axis is bidirectional
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the crosstalk: host immunity regulates the microbiome, and the microbiome modulates host immune responses, creating a feedback loop relevant to cancer surveillance and therapy.

content_rubrics.title: Timing of antibiotic exposure relative to ICI initiation is critical
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the negative association on survival is strongest when antibiotics are administered in the peri-initiation window (shortly before or after starting ICI), highlighting the importance of timing.

question: Among patients with solid tumors treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors, how strongly do baseline tumor mutational burden and CD8+ T-cell infiltration each predict objective response, and are they additive or overlapping predictors?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: TMB as a strong genomic predictor of response
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB) is consistently reported as one of the strongest single genomic predictors of objective response to immune checkpoint inhibitors across multiple solid tumor types, as evidenced by large pan-cancer analyses.

content_rubrics.title: Clonal TMB vs. total TMB predictive strength
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that clonal TMB has been identified as a stronger predictor of CPI response than total TMB, and that both are stronger predictors than microenvironmental transcriptomic markers.

content_rubrics.title: CD8+ T-effector phenotype association with response
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that response to PD-L1 blockade (e.g., atezolizumab) is associated with a CD8+ T-effector cell phenotype, as demonstrated in metastatic urothelial cancer.

content_rubrics.title: Comparative strength: TMB vs. CD8+ infiltration
content_rubrics.description: The answer should directly compare the predictive strength, stating that in specific studies (e.g., Mariathasan et al., 2018), high neoantigen/TMB was associated with response 'to an even greater extent' than the CD8+ T-effector phenotype, indicating TMB may be a stronger individual predictor.

content_rubrics.title: TMB and T-cell-inflamed GEP as joint predictors
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that TMB and an inflammatory/T-cell-inflamed gene-expression profile (GEP) provide 'joint predictive utility' when combined, suggesting they are additive rather than purely overlapping predictors.

content_rubrics.title: Immune exclusion mediated by TGFβ signaling
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe that lack of response is associated with TGFβ signaling in fibroblasts, leading to exclusion of CD8+ T cells from the tumor parenchyma, which is a resistance mechanism distinct from TMB levels.

question: In individuals with ALS, how do antisense oligonucleotide therapies targeting known ALS genes compare with standard of care in slowing functional decline as measured by ALSFRS-R?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Tofersen Phase 3 Primary Endpoint Result
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that in the VALOR phase 3 trial, tofersen did not meet its primary endpoint, showing no statistically significant difference in ALSFRS-R decline versus placebo at 28 weeks in the faster-progression subgroup (-6.98 vs -8.14; difference 1.2 points, 95% CI -3.2 to 5.5).

content_rubrics.title: Tofersen Secondary Clinical Endpoints
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that results for secondary clinical endpoints (e.g., slow vital capacity, handheld dynamometry) in the VALOR trial did not differ significantly between the tofersen and placebo groups at the 28-week timepoint.

content_rubrics.title: Tofersen Open-Label Extension (OLE) ALSFRS-R Result
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the ALSFRS-R results from the combined analysis at 52 weeks, showing a smaller decline in the early-start cohort compared to the delayed-start cohort (-6.0 vs -9.5; difference 3.5 points, 95% CI 0.4 to 6.7).

content_rubrics.title: Tofersen's Effect on Biomarkers
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that tofersen successfully reduced its target biomarkers, specifically leading to greater reductions in CSF SOD1 protein and plasma neurofilament light chain concentrations compared to placebo.

content_rubrics.title: Interpretation of OLE Data Limitations
content_rubrics.description: The model should acknowledge that the open-label extension comparison (early vs delayed start) is not a fully randomized comparison and is subject to the limitations of such study designs when interpreting its suggested benefit.

content_rubrics.title: Safety Profile of Tofersen
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the adverse events associated with tofersen, specifically that lumbar puncture-related events were common and neurologic serious adverse events occurred in 7% of recipients.

question: In inherited metabolic liver diseases, how does ex vivo gene-corrected hepatocyte transplantation compare with in vivo gene therapy in terms of metabolic correction and long-term engraftment?
topic: Cell therapy vs gene therapy for metabolic disorders

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Ex vivo approach enables large-scale hepatocyte expansion
content_rubrics.description: The model's answer should mention that a key advantage of the ex vivo approach is the ability to expand patient-derived hepatocytes on a large scale in culture prior to gene correction and transplantation, as demonstrated in the 2024 Cell Stem Cell paper.

content_rubrics.title: Ex vivo corrected hepatocytes can repopulate from ectopic sites
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that ex vivo gene-corrected hepatocytes transplanted into ectopic sites (e.g., lymph nodes) can engraft, form functional tissue, and subsequently migrate to repopulate the native diseased liver, as shown in the porcine HT1 model (PMID: 32913881).

content_rubrics.title: Ex vivo CRISPR correction efficiency with AAV variant
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that efficient CRISPR-Cas9-mediated gene correction in proliferating patient-derived hepatocytes for diseases like FAH and OTC deficiency was achieved using a specific AAV variant (AAV2.7m8), as reported in the 2024 study.

content_rubrics.title: Metabolic cure in large animal model via ex vivo lentiviral correction
content_rubrics.description: The answer must include that ex vivo lentiviral gene correction of autologous hepatocytes and transplantation led to a clinical cure of acute liver failure and hereditary tyrosinemia type 1 (HT1) in a large animal (porcine) model, with durable engraftment observed for up to 8 months (PMID: 32913881).

content_rubrics.title: Ex vivo strategy combines expansion, editing, and high repopulation
content_rubrics.description: The model should describe the integrated ex vivo strategy that combines large-scale cell expansion, efficient gene editing, and results in high repopulation levels in an injured mouse liver, successfully treating tyrosinemia (as per the 2024 paper).

content_rubrics.title: Lymph nodes as a hepatocyte reservoir for native liver repopulation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that for the ex vivo approach, transplanted lymph nodes can serve as an engraftment site and a reservoir from which a subpopulation of corrected hepatocytes migrates to repopulate the native diseased liver, based on integration site analysis from the porcine study.

content_rubrics.title: Acute phase efficacy comparison LAI vs oral
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that, according to the network meta-analysis of acute phase RCTs (PMID: 38490016), there were no significant efficacy differences between LAIs and oral formulations for antipsychotics like risperidone, paliperidone, aripiprazole, and olanzapine.

content_rubrics.title: Relapse prevention efficacy in RCTs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should address the finding from the network meta-analyses (PMID: 38215784) that combined RCT and real-world data, specifically stating the comparative efficacy of antipsychotics (including LAIs vs oral) for relapse prevention in schizophrenia.

content_rubrics.title: Side effect profile comparison LAI vs oral
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the finding from the acute phase systematic review (PMID: 38490016) that some side effects were less frequent under LAIs than under their oral counterparts.

content_rubrics.title: Metabolic adverse effects in comparative studies
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss whether the retrieved papers provide specific comparative data on metabolic adverse effect profiles (e.g., weight gain, glucose dysregulation, lipid changes) between LAIs and their oral counterparts from RCT evidence.

content_rubrics.title: Generalizability of RCT findings to real-world
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge the debate highlighted in PMID: 38215784 regarding the generalizability of RCT results to real-world settings for antipsychotic efficacy and effectiveness in relapse prevention.

content_rubrics.title: Confidence level in the comparative evidence
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note the assessed confidence in the evidence from the network meta-analysis (PMID: 38490016), which was reported as moderate for most comparisons between LAIs and oral formulations.

content_rubrics.title: Overall survival hazard ratio from primary analysis
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the hazard ratio (HR) for death from the primary analysis of IMbrave150, comparing atezolizumab+bevacizumab to sorafenib, which was 0.58 (95% CI 0.42–0.79).

content_rubrics.title: Updated median overall survival comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state the updated median overall survival times from the longer follow-up analysis: 19.2 months for atezolizumab+bevacizumab versus 13.4 months for sorafenib.

content_rubrics.title: Progression-free survival hazard ratio from primary analysis
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the hazard ratio (HR) for disease progression or death from the primary analysis, which was 0.59 (95% CI 0.47–0.76), favoring atezolizumab+bevacizumab.

content_rubrics.title: Updated progression-free survival results
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the updated median progression-free survival results: 6.9 months for atezolizumab+bevacizumab versus 4.3 months for sorafenib, with an HR of 0.65 (95% CI 0.53–0.81).

content_rubrics.title: Grade 3 or 4 adverse event rates
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note the comparable rates of Grade 3 or 4 adverse events between the two treatment groups as reported in the primary analysis (56.5% for atezolizumab+bevacizumab vs. 55.1% for sorafenib).

content_rubrics.description: Given the user's question on bleeding risk, the answer should specifically mention the Grade 3 or 4 hypertension rate (15.2%) in the atezolizumab+bevacizumab group as a key adverse event related to bevacizumab's mechanism.

question: What mechanisms of primary resistance to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade are most consistently supported in human tumor studies, particularly involving antigen presentation defects and interferon signaling alterations?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: JAK1/2 mutations impair IFN-γ response and PD-L1 induction
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that loss-of-function mutations in JAK1 or JAK2, as identified in human tumor studies, are a key mechanism of primary resistance. These mutations render tumor cells unable to properly respond to IFN-γ, leading to a failure to upregulate PD-L1 and other interferon-stimulated genes, which is critical for effective anti-tumor immunity.

content_rubrics.title: IFN-γ signaling defects occur despite high tumor mutational burden
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that JAK1/2-inactivating mutations and the resulting IFN-γ signaling defects are observed in pretreatment tumors from patients who are non-responders to PD-1 blockade, even in cases where the tumor has a high mutational load (TMB), highlighting this as a primary resistance mechanism independent of antigen load.

content_rubrics.title: Antigen presentation by mast cells influences PD-1 response
content_rubrics.description: The model should describe the role of antigen-presenting mast cells (apMCs) in the tumor microenvironment, as supported by human studies. High levels of apMCs, particularly within tertiary lymphoid structures, are associated with enhanced efficacy of PD-1 blockade in triple-negative breast cancer, linking antigen presentation capacity to treatment outcome.

content_rubrics.title: Loss of antigen presentation machinery in mast cells dampens T cells
content_rubrics.description: The model should note the mechanistic evidence from human tumor studies that conditional deletion of antigen-presenting machinery in mast cells leads to a reduction in tumor-reactive T cell activity, directly connecting defects in antigen presentation by specific immune cells to impaired anti-tumor immunity.

content_rubrics.title: Local IFN-γ delivery can overcome primary resistance to PD-1 blockade
content_rubrics.description: The model should reference studies demonstrating that localized, intratumoral delivery of IFN-γ (e.g., via engineered bacteria) can overcome primary resistance to PD-1 blockade by activating cytotoxic CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, providing functional evidence that restoring this signaling pathway can bypass tumor-intrinsic resistance mechanisms.

question: In ALS, what mechanistic links have been identified between TDP-43 pathology and nucleocytoplasmic transport defects in motor neurons?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: TDP-43 aggregates sequester nuclear pore complex components
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that detergent-insoluble TDP-43 aggregates are enriched for nuclear pore complex (NPC) proteins and nucleocytoplasmic transport factors, as identified by proximity-dependent biotin identification (BioID), leading to their sequestration and mislocalization.

content_rubrics.title: TDP-43 pathology disrupts nuclear protein import and RNA export
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that aggregated and mutant TDP-43 interferes with nuclear protein import and RNA export in relevant cellular models, including mouse primary cortical neurons and human iPSC-derived neurons.

content_rubrics.title: Nuclear pore pathology is present in human ALS/FTD tissue
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that nuclear pore pathology is observed in brain tissue from both sporadic ALS cases and cases with genetic mutations in TARDBP and C9orf72, linking TDP-43 pathology to NCT defects in human disease.

content_rubrics.title: Poly(GR) from C9orf72 expansions induces TDP-43 aggregation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that poly(glycine-arginine) [poly(GR)] proteins translated from C9orf72 G4C2 repeat expansions are sufficient to promote the aggregation of endogenous TDP-43, providing a mechanistic link between C9orf72 mutations and TDP-43 pathology.

content_rubrics.title: Poly(GR) causes mislocalization of nucleocytoplasmic transport factors
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe that poly(GR) proteins cause the mislocalization of nucleocytoplasmic transport factors and nuclear pore complex proteins, as demonstrated in GFP-(GR)200 mouse models.

content_rubrics.title: Poly(GR)-induced NCT defects lead to TDP-43 cytoplasmic accumulation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should connect the poly(GR)-induced mislocalization of NCT machinery to the subsequent aberrant accumulation and co-aggregation of endogenous TDP-43 in the cytoplasm.

content_rubrics.title: Antisense oligonucleotide treatment reduces poly(GR) and TDP-43 pathology
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that treating G4C2 repeat-expressing mice with repeat-targeting antisense oligonucleotides lowers poly(GR) burden, which is accompanied by reduced TDP-43 pathology and neurodegeneration.

content_rubrics.title: Use of group sequential designs with alpha-spending functions
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that group sequential designs with alpha-spending functions (e.g., Lan-DeMets) are a common strategy to control Type I error across multiple interim analyses. It should explain that this approach allocates or 'spends' the overall alpha level across planned looks, preserving the family-wise error rate.

content_rubrics.title: Application of multiplicity control for co-primary endpoints
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe how statistical plans for trials with multiple endpoints (e.g., recurrence-free survival and overall survival) incorporate formal multiplicity control. content_rubrics.title: Pre-specification of interim analysis timing and criteria
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the timing of interim analyses (e.g., first and second interim) is pre-planned based on specific event counts (e.g., after approximately 128 and 179 events). This pre-specification is a key part of the design to maintain error control and interpretability.

content_rubrics.title: Impact of early stopping on result maturity and follow-up
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss how stopping at an interim analysis, while statistically valid, results in shorter median follow-up times (e.g., 14.4 months). This impacts the interpretability of results, as longer-term efficacy and safety data are less mature compared to a full trial duration.

content_rubrics.title: UCART19 CRS frequency and severity in B-ALL
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report that in UCART19 studies for pediatric/adult B-ALL, CRS occurred in 91% of patients, with grade 3-4 CRS reported in 14% of patients, as per Benjamin et al. (2020).

content_rubrics.title: UCART19 neurotoxicity frequency and severity in B-ALL
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report that in UCART19 studies, neurotoxicity occurred in 38% of patients, with all reported events being grade 1-2 (no high-grade neurotoxicity), as per Benjamin et al. (2020).

content_rubrics.title: Clinical consequences of UCART19 toxicity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that in the UCART19 studies, there were two treatment-related deaths, including one in a patient with neutropenic sepsis and concurrent CRS, as reported by Benjamin et al. (2020).

content_rubrics.title: Cema-cel/ALLO-501 CRS frequency and severity in LBCL
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report that in the ALPHA/ALPHA2 studies for LBCL, no cases of grade ≥3 cytokine release syndrome were observed with cemacabtagene ansegedleucel/ALLO-501, as per Locke et al. (2025).

content_rubrics.title: Cema-cel/ALLO-501 neurotoxicity (ICANS) frequency
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report that in the ALPHA/ALPHA2 studies, no cases of immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) were reported with cemacabtagene ansegedleucel/ALLO-501, as per Locke et al. (2025).

content_rubrics.title: Comparison of high-grade CRS rates
content_rubrics.description: The answer should contrast the reported incidence of grade 3-4 CRS (14% in UCART19) with the absence of grade ≥3 CRS in cema-cel/ALLO-501, based on the provided papers.

content_rubrics.title: Comparison of neurotoxicity profiles
content_rubrics.description: The answer should compare the reported neurotoxicity profiles, specifically the 38% incidence (all grade 1-2) in UCART19 versus the reported absence of ICANS in cema-cel/ALLO-501, based on the provided papers.

question: In metastatic urothelial carcinoma, does maintenance avelumab after response or stable disease on platinum chemotherapy improve overall survival compared with observation or best supportive care?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Trial name and design
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the evidence comes from the phase III JAVELIN Bladder 100 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT02603432), which randomly assigned patients 1:1 to receive avelumab plus best supportive care (BSC) versus BSC alone.

content_rubrics.title: Patient eligibility criteria
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that patients had unresectable locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma who did not have disease progression after 4-6 cycles of first-line platinum chemotherapy (gemcitabine plus cisplatin or carboplatin).

content_rubrics.title: Primary endpoint improvement
content_rubrics.description: The model should report that overall survival (OS) improved with avelumab maintenance, including specific metrics: median OS 21.4 months vs 14.3 months, hazard ratio 0.69 (95% CI 0.56-0.86), P=0.001, and 1-year OS 71.3% vs 58.4%.

content_rubrics.title: Longer-term follow-up confirmation
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that with ≥2 years of follow-up, the overall survival benefit was maintained with hazard ratio 0.76 (95% CI 0.63-0.91), P=0.0036, confirming sustained efficacy.

content_rubrics.title: Treatment group sizes
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that each treatment group had 350 patients (n = 350 for avelumab plus BSC, n = 350 for BSC alone), with median follow-up of 38.0 months and 39.6 months respectively.

content_rubrics.title: Safety assessment
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that longer-term safety was consistent with previous analyses and no new safety signals were identified with longer treatment duration.

content_rubrics.title: Standard of care status
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that avelumab first-line maintenance treatment is now a standard of care for advanced urothelial carcinoma based on these trial results.

content_rubrics.title: Response durability in older patients with ide-cel
content_rubrics.description: For BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel) in older patients with multiple myeloma, the median progression-free survival was 9.1 months with median follow-up of 14.2 months, and overall survival was 26.5 months.

content_rubrics.title: Cytokine release syndrome severity in older ide-cel patients
content_rubrics.description: For BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel) in older patients, grade ≥3 cytokine-release syndrome was observed in only 1% of patients, which is lower than the 5% grade ≥3 CRS rate reported in the model's answer.

content_rubrics.title: Overall response rate for ide-cel in Japanese RRMM patients
content_rubrics.description: For BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel) in Japanese patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma, the overall response rate was 89% with median follow-up of 12.9 months.

content_rubrics.title: Response durability metrics in Japanese RRMM cohort
content_rubrics.description: In the Japanese cohort study of BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel), median duration of response was not reached, indicating potentially durable responses in this specific population.

content_rubrics.title: Cytokine release syndrome severity in Japanese RRMM patients
content_rubrics.description: For BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel) in Japanese patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma, all patients experienced grade ≤2 cytokine release syndrome, indicating no severe CRS in this cohort.

content_rubrics.title: Comparison of frailty characteristics in older vs younger patients
content_rubrics.description: Older patients receiving BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel) had significantly higher prevalence of frailty (66.7%), polypharmacy (97% with ≥5 drugs), ≥4 comorbidities (69%), and organ dysfunction (35%) compared to younger patients.

content_rubrics.title: Safety and efficacy similarity across age groups
content_rubrics.description: For BCMA-directed CAR-T (ide-cel), safety and efficacy were similar in younger and older patients despite differences in frailty and geriatric characteristics in older patients.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that loss-of-function mutations in the interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) signaling pathway are a primary tumor-intrinsic resistance mechanism to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade, as evidenced by studies showing such mutations can cause acquired resistance.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that defects in the antigen presentation pathway are a primary tumor-intrinsic resistance mechanism to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade, as genomic studies have revealed them as a constraint on T cell therapy efficacy.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that IFN-γ can overcome resistance by activating natural killer (NK) cells, which can target tumors with defective IFN-γ signaling or antigen presentation.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference that defects in antigen presentation and interferon response pathways are identified as resistance mechanisms in patients with anti-PD-1 refractory epithelial cancers.

content_rubrics.title: Upadacitinib vs adalimumab remission rates at 5 years
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite the specific Clinical Disease Activity Index (CDAI) remission (≤2.8) and DAS28(CRP) <2.6 rates from the SELECT-COMPARE 5-year data, showing upadacitinib was numerically superior (e.g., 24.6% vs 18.7% for CDAI remission).

content_rubrics.title: Safety comparison: infection rates
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the safety findings from the SELECT-COMPARE studies, specifically noting that rates of treatment-emergent adverse events were generally similar, but herpes zoster rates were numerically higher with upadacitinib compared to adalimumab.

content_rubrics.title: Sustained remission at 3 years
content_rubrics.description: The model should reference the SELECT-COMPARE 3-year data indicating that sustained clinical responses and remission were numerically better with upadacitinib compared to adalimumab in patients with inadequate methotrexate response.

content_rubrics.title: Study design context for remission data
content_rubrics.description: The model should acknowledge that the remission data from SELECT-COMPARE are influenced by the trial design, including non-responder imputation (NRI) analysis and rescue therapy rules (e.g., switching at week 26), which affect the interpretation of sustained remission rates.

content_rubrics.title: Comparison class: TNF inhibitor used
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that the primary TNF inhibitor comparator in the cited evidence (SELECT-COMPARE) is adalimumab (40 mg every other week), not the entire TNF inhibitor class.

content_rubrics.title: Patient population definition
content_rubrics.description: The model should explicitly state that the evidence applies to adults with rheumatoid arthritis who have had an inadequate response to methotrexate (MTX-IR), and that treatments were added to background methotrexate therapy.

question: How do hepatic de novo lipogenesis and adipose tissue lipolysis each contribute to intrahepatic triglyceride accumulation in NAFLD, and what biomarkers best reflect these pathways in humans?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Primary source of hepatic fatty acids in NAFLD
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that circulating non-esterified fatty acids (NEFAs), largely derived from adipose tissue lipolysis, are the dominant quantitative source of fatty acids for intrahepatic triglyceride accumulation in NAFLD.

content_rubrics.title: Role of insulin resistance in adipose tissue lipolysis
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that insulin-resistant adipose tissue is less effectively suppressed by insulin, leading to higher basal and postprandial lipolysis, which increases NEFA delivery to the liver.

content_rubrics.title: Contribution of hepatic de novo lipogenesis (DNL)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe hepatic DNL as a substantial, often upregulated contributor that converts carbohydrate-derived carbon (e.g., from sugars/fructose) into fatty acids, which are then esterified into hepatic triglycerides.

content_rubrics.title: Metabolic fate of liver-derived fatty acids
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that fatty acids synthesized via DNL, along with those from other sources, are incorporated into triacylglycerol, stored in lipid droplets, or secreted as VLDL particles.

content_rubrics.title: Fasting state and adipose-liver axis
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that fasting promotes adipose tissue lipolysis, releasing NEFAs to the liver, linking adipose tissue function directly to hepatic lipid metabolism.

content_rubrics.title: Integration of gut and adipose signals in liver metabolism
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that the liver integrates signals from the gastrointestinal tract (e.g., dietary substrates) and adipose tissue (NEFAs) to regulate lipid metabolism, with dysregulation leading to steatotic liver disease.

question: Among hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 requiring oxygen, what is the comparative effectiveness of tocilizumab versus baricitinib when added to corticosteroids for reducing mortality and progression to mechanical ventilation?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Head-to-head RCT primary outcome result
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the primary composite outcome (mechanical ventilation or death by day 28) from the Karampitsakos et al. RCT, specifically the event rates for baricitinib (39.2%) and tocilizumab (44.4%) and the hazard ratio (HR 0.83; 95% CI 0.56–1.21).

content_rubrics.title: Non-inferiority conclusion from direct comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that the Karampitsakos RCT concluded baricitinib was non-inferior to tocilizumab for the primary outcome of mechanical ventilation or death by day 28.

content_rubrics.title: RECOVERY trial baricitinib mortality benefit
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that in the RECOVERY trial, baricitinib added to usual care (which included corticosteroids in 95% of patients) reduced 28-day mortality (12% vs 14%; rate ratio 0.87; 95% CI 0.77–0.99; p=0.028).

content_rubrics.title: RECOVERY trial patient population context
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that in the RECOVERY trial, at the time of randomization, 95% of patients were receiving corticosteroids and 23% were receiving tocilizumab, establishing the context of baricitinib's additive effect to standard care.

content_rubrics.title: RECOVERY trial meta-analysis result
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the updated meta-analysis result from the RECOVERY paper, which showed allocation to a JAK inhibitor (chiefly baricitinib) was associated with a 20% reduction in mortality (rate ratio 0.80; 95% CI 0.72–0.89; p<0.0001).

content_rubrics.title: Comparative effectiveness summary statement
content_rubrics.description: The model should synthesize the evidence to conclude that direct head-to-head evidence shows baricitinib is non-inferior to tocilizumab, while large platform trials show each independently adds benefit on top of corticosteroids.

all_rubrics.title: High baseline IL-6 and incidence of severe CRS
all_rubrics.description: The model should specify that in the study (PMID: 37153543), the incidence of severe CRS was significantly higher in patients with high pre-infusion IL-6 (26%) compared to those with low IL-6 (9%), with a P-value of 0.0405.

all_rubrics.title: Baseline CRP's prognostic role for overall survival
all_rubrics.description: The model should note that the retrieved paper (PMID: 37153543) identified high pre-infusion CRP as significantly associated with inferior overall survival (HR, 2.043; 95% CI, 1.019 to 4.097; P = .044), establishing its prognostic, if not directly predictive, relevance.

all_rubrics.title: Composite inflammatory prognostic index (InPI)
all_rubrics.description: The model should describe that the study (PMID: 37153543) developed an Inflammatory Prognostic Index (InPI) combining pre-infusion ferritin, CRP, and IL-6, which stratified patients into risk groups and correlated with toxicity outcomes including CRS severity.

all_rubrics.title: Focus on multiple myeloma patient cohort
