content_rubrics.title: NIV survival benefit by bulbar function
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the randomized trial (Bourke et al., 2006) found NIV extended median survival by approximately 205 days compared to standard care, but this significant survival benefit was observed specifically in the subgroup of patients with normal to moderately impaired bulbar function.

content_rubrics.title: NIV impact on QoL by bulbar function
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that for patients with better bulbar function, NIV improved multiple validated quality-of-life measures and maintained QoL for most of the extended survival period. For patients with poor bulbar function, NIV improved some sleep-related quality-of-life indices but did not maintain QoL to the same extent.

content_rubrics.title: Cochrane overview conclusion on NIV efficacy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the Cochrane overview (Ng et al., 2017) judged the evidence as moderate-quality, concluding that NIV probably improves both survival and quality of life in ALS patients with respiratory insufficiency and normal to moderately impaired bulbar function.

content_rubrics.title: NIV for severe bulbar impairment
content_rubrics.description: The answer must clarify, based on Bourke et al. (2006), that in patients with severe bulbar impairment, NIV improved sleep-related symptoms but did not confer a large survival advantage compared to standard care.

content_rubrics.title: Specific QoL measures improved by NIV
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the specific primary quality-of-life outcome measures improved by NIV in the Bourke et al. (2006) trial: the Short Form 36 (SF-36) mental component summary (MCS) and the Sleep Apnoea Quality of Life Index symptoms domain.

content_rubrics.title: Comparative benefit of NIV vs neuroprotective therapy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the finding from Bourke et al. (2006) that the survival benefit from NIV in patients without severe bulbar dysfunction is stated to be 'much greater than that from currently available neuroprotective therapy'.

question: In COVID-19 patients at high risk of thromboembolism, what is the net clinical benefit of therapeutic-dose anticoagulation compared with prophylactic-dose anticoagulation in preventing thrombotic events without increasing major bleeding?

content_rubrics.title: HEP-COVID patient population definition
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that HEP-COVID enrolled hospitalized COVID-19 patients defined as high-risk based on D-dimer >4x ULN or sepsis-induced coagulopathy (SIC) score ≥4.

content_rubrics.title: Primary efficacy outcome components
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the primary efficacy outcome in HEP-COVID was a composite of venous thromboembolism (VTE), arterial thromboembolism (ATE), or death from any cause at 30 days.

content_rubrics.title: Absolute reduction in thromboembolism rate
content_rubrics.description: The answer must report the absolute rates of thromboembolism (VTE/ATE) from HEP-COVID: 29.0% with standard prophylactic/intermediate-dose vs. 10.9% with therapeutic-dose anticoagulation, resulting in an 18.1% absolute risk reduction.

content_rubrics.title: Absolute increase in major bleeding rate
content_rubrics.description: The answer must report the absolute rates of major bleeding from HEP-COVID: 1.6% with standard-dose vs. 4.7% with therapeutic-dose heparins, resulting in a 3.1% absolute risk increase.

content_rubrics.title: Statistical significance of primary outcome
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the reduction in the primary composite efficacy outcome (VTE/ATE/death) with therapeutic-dose LMWH in HEP-COVID was statistically significant (RR 0.68, 95% CI 0.49-0.96, P=0.03).

content_rubrics.title: Differential effect in ICU vs. non-ICU patients
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note the HEP-COVID finding that the primary efficacy benefit was observed in non-ICU patients (RR 0.46, P=0.004) but not in ICU patients (RR 0.92, P=0.71).

content_rubrics.title: Statistical non-significance of bleeding difference
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the observed increase in major bleeding with therapeutic dosing in HEP-COVID was not statistically significant (RR 2.88, 95% CI 0.59-14.02, P=0.17).

content_rubrics.title: Lecanemab Phase 2b 18-month Clinical Benefit
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that in the lecanemab (BAN2401) Phase 2b trial, prespecified 18-month analyses showed a reduction in clinical decline versus placebo, as measured by ADCOMS (27-30%), ADAS-Cog14 (47-56%), and CDR-SB (26-33%).

content_rubrics.title: Lecanemab Phase 3 CDR-SB Primary Outcome
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state the specific, statistically significant difference in Clinical Dementia Rating-Sum-of-Boxes (CDR-SB) score change at 18 months between lecanemab and placebo from the Phase 3 Clarity AD trial, which is a difference of -0.45 points.

content_rubrics.title: Donanemab Phase 3 CDR-SB Efficacy in Low/Medium Tau
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the specific magnitude of clinical benefit for donanemab versus placebo on the CDR-SB scale in the low/medium tau population from the TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 trial, which is a difference of approximately -0.67 points.

content_rubrics.title: Amyloid Reduction with Lecanemab Treatment
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include that lecanemab treatment (10 mg/kg biweekly) led to a reduction in brain amyloid plaque burden, quantified as a decrease of -0.306 SUVr units on PET scan at 18 months in the Phase 2b trial.

content_rubrics.title: Incidence of ARIA-E with Lecanemab
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify the incidence rate of amyloid-related imaging abnormalities with edema/effusion (ARIA-E) for the 10 mg/kg biweekly lecanemab dose in the Phase 2b trial, which was 9.9%.

content_rubrics.title: Patient Population in Lecanemab Phase 2b Trial
content_rubrics.description: The answer should correctly identify the patient population enrolled in the lecanemab Phase 2b trial (BAN2401-G000-201) as individuals with early Alzheimer's disease, specifically mild cognitive impairment due to AD and mild AD dementia.

content_rubrics.title: tREACH trial 1-year radiographic outcome
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the tREACH trial found no difference in radiographic progression at 1 year between initial triple DMARD therapy and MTX monotherapy (both with glucocorticoid bridging), with progression occurring in approximately 21-24% of patients across arms.

content_rubrics.title: TEAR trial MTX-first strategy outcome
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the TEAR trial showed starting with MTX monotherapy and stepping up if needed produced similar radiographic outcomes to immediate combination therapy at 2 years, and that MTX responders who stayed on monotherapy had very low radiographic progression.

content_rubrics.title: NORD-STAR conventional arm radiographic comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the NORD-STAR non-randomised subgroup analysis showing that at 48 weeks, the triple therapy plus intra-articular glucocorticoids group had a slightly higher adjusted mean change in van der Heijde-modified Sharp Score (0.80) compared to the MTX plus oral prednisolone group (0.26), indicating slightly worse radiographic outcomes with the triple therapy regimen in this study.

content_rubrics.title: Context of glucocorticoid use in comparisons
content_rubrics.description: The model should acknowledge that the comparisons between triple therapy and MTX monotherapy in the cited trials (tREACH, NORD-STAR) involve different concomitant glucocorticoid strategies (bridging, oral, or intra-articular), which is a key contextual factor for interpreting radiographic outcomes.

content_rubrics.title: SCFAs as signaling molecules via GPCRs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) act as signaling ligands through G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs), such as GPR41/43, which is a key mechanism by which they influence host metabolism, as discussed in the retrieved reviews.

content_rubrics.title: SCFA impact on appetite and energy expenditure
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include that SCFAs modulate host metabolic health through tissue-specific mechanisms related to appetite regulation and energy expenditure, a point explicitly covered in the human-focused review (PMID: 32865024).

content_rubrics.title: Limited direct human evidence for SCFA mechanisms
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that while mechanistic data on SCFAs is strong, well-controlled human intervention studies are limited and more are urgently needed, a key caveat highlighted in both retrieved papers (PMID: 32865024, 26963409).

content_rubrics.title: Microbial SCFA production from indigestible carbohydrates
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that acetate, propionate, and butyrate are produced from the microbial fermentation of indigestible carbohydrates (e.g., dietary fiber), which is the foundational source of these metabolites as per the retrieved papers.

content_rubrics.title: SCFAs and gut integrity maintenance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that SCFAs are essential for maintaining gut integrity by regulating luminal pH, mucus production, and providing fuel for epithelial cells, a role described in the context of gut health (PMID: 32865024).

content_rubrics.title: SCFAs as major carbon flux from diet to host
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the concept that SCFAs represent a major carbon flux from the diet through the gut microbiota to the host, underpinning their significant role in metabolism as indicated in the review (PMID: 26963409).

question: How does the gut microbiome composition relate to clinical response and immune-related toxicity in patients treated with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors across solid tumors?

content_rubrics.title: Bacteroides intestinalis link to combined ICB toxicity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that a higher abundance of Bacteroides intestinalis in the gut microbiome is significantly associated with immune-related toxicity (specifically colitis) in patients receiving combined CTLA-4 and PD-1 blockade, as identified in a melanoma cohort study.

content_rubrics.title: Microbiome modulation via inulin gel enhances anti-PD-1
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe that in situ modulation of the gut microbiome using an orally administered inulin gel can amplify the antitumour activity of anti-PD-1 therapy in murine models, by increasing beneficial commensals and short-chain fatty acids, leading to enhanced T-cell responses.

content_rubrics.title: Microbial diversity and Ruminococcaceae in anti-PD-1 response
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that in melanoma patients, responders to anti-PD-1 therapy had higher gut microbial alpha diversity and enrichment of Ruminococcaceae, with functional evidence from fecal microbiota transfer experiments supporting a causal role in efficacy.

content_rubrics.title: Specific commensals enriched in anti-PD-1/PD-L1 responders
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that baseline enrichment of commensal bacteria such as Bifidobacterium longum, Collinsella aerofaciens, and Enterococcus faecium has been associated with clinical response to PD-1/PD-L1 axis therapy, supported by mouse model transfer experiments.

content_rubrics.title: Distinct microbial signatures for efficacy vs. toxicity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the microbial signatures associated with clinical response (e.g., Ruminococcaceae, Bifidobacterium) are not identical to those linked to toxicity (e.g., Bacteroides intestinalis), indicating partially overlapping but distinct pathways.

content_rubrics.title: BAT-derived MaR2 resolves obesity-induced inflammation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that cold exposure and β3-adrenergic stimulation promote brown adipose tissue to produce maresin 2 (MaR2), a specialized pro-resolving mediator that contributes to resolving obesity-induced inflammation and improving insulin resistance, as demonstrated in the 2022 Nature metabolism paper.

content_rubrics.title: BAT activation improves insulin sensitivity via multiple organs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the beneficial effects of cold exposure on improving obesity-induced inflammation and insulin resistance depend on both brown adipose tissue and the liver, indicating a systemic mechanism beyond local thermogenesis, as supported by the 2022 paper.

content_rubrics.title: BAT and beige fat as therapeutic targets for T2DM
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that activating BAT and inducing the browning of white adipose tissue (WAT) is considered a novel therapeutic strategy for type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) by improving glycolipid metabolism and insulin resistance, as reviewed in the 2021 Adipocyte paper.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism: Reduced insulin secretion requirement
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that a proposed mechanism by which BAT activation improves metabolic health is by accelerating the intake of glycolipids, thereby reducing the insulin secretion requirement from the pancreas, which can alleviate insulin resistance in obese and T2DM patients, as per the 2021 review.

content_rubrics.title: Browning agents as a pharmacologic strategy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss the role of 'browning agents' as a pharmacologic strategy to activate BAT or induce beiging of WAT for the treatment of obesity and T2DM, as a key focus of the 2021 therapeutic review.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that mRNA vaccination induces significantly lower levels of neutralizing antibodies against Omicron BA.1.1 in the bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BAL) compared to COVID-19 convalescents, indicating weak respiratory mucosal neutralizing antibody responses against Omicron.

content_rubrics.title: Limited protection against breakthrough infection by Omicron
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that current COVID-19 vaccines offer limited protection against breakthrough infection, especially by the Omicron sublineage, based on the finding that systemic mRNA vaccination alone induces weak mucosal neutralizing responses against Omicron.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the finding that a combination of systemic mRNA vaccination plus mucosal adenovirus-S immunization induces strong neutralizing antibody responses against the Omicron BA.1.1 variant, suggesting mucosal booster vaccination is needed for robust sterilizing immunity against Omicron.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the Omicron variant caused a drastic decrease of plasma neutralizing activity elicited by vaccination or prior infection, highlighting its enhanced evasion capability.

content_rubrics.title: Pembrolizumab vs placebo 7-year RFS hazard ratio
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the recurrence-free survival hazard ratio (HR) from the 7-year follow-up of the KEYNOTE-054/EORTC 1325 trial, which is HR 0.63 (95% CI 0.53 to 0.74), as per PMID 39288737.

content_rubrics.title: Pembrolizumab vs placebo 7-year absolute RFS rates
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the 7-year recurrence-free survival rates from the KEYNOTE-054/EORTC 1325 trial: 50% (95% CI 46% to 55%) for pembrolizumab versus 36% (95% CI 32% to 41%) for placebo, resulting in an absolute difference of +14%, as per PMID 39288737.

content_rubrics.title: Pembrolizumab vs placebo 3-year RFS hazard ratio
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the recurrence-free survival hazard ratio (HR) from the earlier (3-year) follow-up of the KEYNOTE-054/EORTC 1325 trial, which was HR 0.56, as referenced in the model's answer citing PMID 32946353.

content_rubrics.title: Pembrolizumab vs placebo 3-year absolute RFS rates
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the 3-year recurrence-free survival rates from the KEYNOTE-054/EORTC 1325 trial: 63.7% for pembrolizumab versus 44.1% for placebo, resulting in an absolute difference of +19.6%, as referenced in the model's answer citing PMID 32946353.

content_rubrics.title: Distant metastasis-free survival (DMFS) benefit
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the sustained benefit in distant metastasis-free survival (DMFS) with adjuvant pembrolizumab versus placebo, reporting the 7-year DMFS HR of 0.64 (95% CI 0.54 to 0.76) and rates of 54% vs 42% from PMID 39288737.

content_rubrics.title: Benefit consistency across substages
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the RFS benefit of adjuvant pembrolizumab versus placebo was observed across AJCC-7 substages IIIA, IIIB, and IIIC, as stated in PMID 39288737.

content_rubrics.title: TGF-β signaling in fibroblasts causes T-cell exclusion
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that in metastatic urothelial cancer, lack of response to atezolizumab is associated with a TGF-β signaling signature in fibroblasts, which leads to CD8 T cells being excluded from the tumor parenchyma and retained in the peritumoral stroma.

content_rubrics.title: PD-L1 intrinsic signaling activates IL-6/JAK/STAT3 pathway
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the tumor-cell-intrinsic function of PD-L1, where it binds to and inhibits protein tyrosine phosphatase 1B, leading to activation of Jak2/Stat3 signaling, which in turn promotes the transcription of cytokines like IL-6.

content_rubrics.title: PD-L1 intrinsic signaling enhances MDSC recruitment and function
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that PD-L1-overexpressing tumor cells enhance the migration and immunosuppressive activity of myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), mediated by IL-6 and CXCL1, leading to increased tumor infiltration of MDSCs and Tregs.

content_rubrics.title: High baseline serum IL-6 predicts poor ICI outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that in clinical cohorts, a higher baseline serum IL-6 level was associated with poor clinical outcomes following immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) therapy in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).

content_rubrics.title: IL-6/JAK/STAT3 pathway enriched in PD-L1-high non-responders
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that RNA-seq data from ICI-treated NSCLC patients showed enrichment of the IL-6/Jak/Stat3 pathway and higher IL-6 expression specifically in patients with PD-L1-high tumors who did not respond to treatment.

content_rubrics.title: Combined PD-1 and IL-6 blockade improves efficacy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the preclinical finding that in mouse models, combined blockade of PD-1 and IL-6 was effective in controlling tumors, decreasing MDSCs, and increasing functional CD8 T-cells.

question: What is the comparative effectiveness and tolerability of MAO-B inhibitors versus dopamine agonists as initial monotherapy for early Parkinson’s disease?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: MDS-EBM monotherapy recommendations for early PD
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that, according to the International Parkinson and Movement Disorder Society Evidence-Based Medicine review (2018), both MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline) and non-ergot dopamine agonists are considered clinically useful as initial monotherapy for early Parkinson's disease.

content_rubrics.title: Comparative symptomatic potency
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that MAO-B inhibitors are generally considered to have weaker symptomatic potency compared to dopamine agonists, a point that can be inferred from the context of the Cochrane review discussing the higher need for add-on therapy with MAO-B inhibitors.

content_rubrics.title: Likelihood of requiring add-on therapy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the finding from the Cochrane review (2009) that patients started on MAO-B inhibitors are more likely to require add-on therapy compared to those started on dopamine agonists (with an odds ratio of approximately 2.0).

content_rubrics.title: Long-term patient-rated mobility outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the PD MED trial result showing that, from a patient perspective, mobility scores (PDQ-39) were slightly better with initial MAO-B inhibitor therapy than with dopamine agonist therapy over the long term.

content_rubrics.title: Tolerability and safety considerations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss tolerability differences, noting that while dopamine agonists are associated with specific side effects (e.g., impulse control disorders, edema, somnolence), MAO-B inhibitors have a different and generally favorable side effect profile, though they are not without risks (e.g., potential for serotonin syndrome, although rare).

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism and neuroprotective rationale
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the proposed neuroprotective or disease-modifying rationale for MAO-B inhibitors (reducing oxidative stress), which is a distinct consideration from pure symptomatic effect, as highlighted in the review on MAO-B inhibitors.

question: In people living with HIV who are virologically suppressed, what is the comparative efficacy and safety of switching from a three-drug antiretroviral regimen to a two-drug regimen for maintaining viral suppression?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Efficacy of DTG+RPV switch at 48 weeks
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that switching to dolutegravir plus rilpivirine (DTG+RPV) from a 3-/4-drug regimen was noninferior for maintaining HIV-1 RNA <50 copies/mL at the week 48 primary endpoint in the SWORD-1 and SWORD-2 trials.

content_rubrics.title: Long-term durability of DTG+RPV suppression
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include data on the durable maintenance of virological suppression beyond 48 weeks, specifically referencing the high rates of HIV-1 RNA <50 copies/mL at 100 weeks and 84% suppression at 148 weeks from the SWORD studies.

content_rubrics.title: Low virologic failure rate with DTG+RPV
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the switch to DTG+RPV was associated with a low rate of confirmed virologic withdrawal (CVW) and very limited emergent resistance, as reported in the week 148 analysis of the SWORD trials.

content_rubrics.title: Safety/tolerability advantages of DTG+RPV
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that switching to DTG+RPV was generally well tolerated and associated with more favourable renal and bone parameters compared to remaining on the previous ART regimen, as indicated in the review of the SWORD data.

content_rubrics.title: Patient population for switch eligibility
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify the key eligibility criteria from the SWORD trials: virologically suppressed (HIV-1 RNA <50 copies/mL for ≥6 months), no prior virologic failure, and no documented resistance-associated mutations to the switch components (PI, INSTI, NRTI, NNRTI, or R263K).

content_rubrics.title: Efficacy of DTG/3TC switch from TAF regimens
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the TANGO trial, stating that switching to dolutegravir/lamivudine (DTG/3TC) from a TAF-based 3-/4-drug regimen was noninferior for maintaining suppression through week 144.

question: In metastatic non-small cell lung cancer with actionable EGFR mutations, how does first-line osimertinib compare with first- or second-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors in progression-free survival, overall survival, and central nervous system control?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: FLAURA trial CNS PFS comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the median CNS progression-free survival results from the FLAURA trial preplanned subgroup analysis: Not reached for osimertinib vs. 13.9 months for standard EGFR-TKIs (gefitinib/erlotinib) in patients with baseline CNS lesions (HR 0.48; 95% CI, 0.26 to 0.86; P = .014).

content_rubrics.title: CNS objective response rate comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should report the CNS objective response rates from the FLAURA trial: 91% with osimertinib vs. 68% with standard EGFR-TKIs in patients with ≥1 measurable CNS lesion (OR 4.6; P = .066), and 66% vs. 43% in patients with measurable and/or nonmeasurable CNS lesions (OR 2.5; P = .011).

content_rubrics.title: Reduced risk of CNS progression
content_rubrics.description: The model should state the conclusion from the FLAURA CNS analysis that osimertinib suggests a reduced risk of CNS progression compared to first-generation EGFR-TKIs, based on the lower probability of experiencing a CNS progression event.

content_rubrics.title: Overall survival comparison with first-gen TKIs
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite the overall survival results from the FLAURA trial: Median OS was 38.6 months with osimertinib vs. 31.8 months with gefitinib/erlotinib (HR 0.80; 95% CI, 0.64-1.00; P=0.046).

content_rubrics.title: Progression-free survival comparison with first-gen TKIs
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite the primary PFS results from the FLAURA trial: Median PFS was 18.9 months with osimertinib vs. 10.2 months with gefitinib/erlotinib (HR 0.46; 95% CI, 0.37–0.57; P<0.001).

content_rubrics.title: OS comparison with second-generation TKIs
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the indirect comparison of overall survival between osimertinib and second-generation TKIs (afatinib, dacomitinib) based on network meta-analyses, indicating whether osimertinib showed superior, similar, or inferior OS.

content_rubrics.title: PFS comparison with second-generation TKIs
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the indirect comparison of progression-free survival between osimertinib and second-generation TKIs (afatinib, dacomitinib) based on network meta-analyses, indicating whether osimertinib showed superior, similar, or inferior PFS.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should explicitly state that there are no randomized controlled trials (RCTs) directly comparing SGLT2 inhibitors (SGLT2i) with angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitors (ARNI) in HFrEF, as this is the fundamental limitation of the evidence base.

all_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the key indirect comparison from the network meta-analysis (e.g., Ameri et al., 2022), stating the similar hazard ratios for the composite outcome (CV death/HHF) for SGLT2i and ARNI versus neurohormonal inhibitors (e.g., HR ~0.78-0.79).

all_rubrics.title: Applicability to HFrEF with CKD

question: What is the current evidence that time-restricted eating improves glycemic control and hepatic steatosis in adults with type 2 diabetes or NAFLD compared with usual eating patterns?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: TRE vs. Calorie Restriction for HbA1c Reduction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that in a 6-month RCT, an 8-hour TRE window (without calorie counting) lowered HbA1c by approximately 0.9% compared to a control group, and that this reduction was similar to that achieved by daily calorie restriction (CR).

content_rubrics.title: Weight Loss Efficacy of TRE in T2D
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the 6-month RCT found TRE led to a statistically significant reduction in body weight (-3.56%) compared to controls, while the CR group's weight loss was not statistically significant relative to controls.

content_rubrics.title: Adherence and Spontaneous Caloric Reduction in TRE
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that in the 6-month RCT, participants in the TRE group were adherent to their eating window on most days per week, and that the intervention resulted in a spontaneous mean reduction in daily energy intake (-313 kcal/d).

content_rubrics.title: TRE's Effect on Continuous Glucose Monitoring Metrics
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that a 3-week crossover trial found TRE (10-hour window) improved 24-hour glucose profiles, including lower fasting glucose and more time in normoglycemia as measured by CGM, compared to a ≥14-hour eating schedule.

content_rubrics.title: Limitation: TRE's Effect on Insulin Sensitivity
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the finding from the 3-week crossover trial that, despite improved CGM metrics, TRE did not significantly improve clamp-measured insulin sensitivity in adults with type 2 diabetes over the short study duration.

content_rubrics.title: TRE as a Non-Inferior Alternative Strategy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the conclusion from a 6-month parallel RCT that TRE can be an effective alternative behavioral strategy for glycemic control, showing non-inferiority to individualized dietetic guidance when standard counseling is not available.

content_rubrics.title: Safety Profile of TRE in T2D Trials
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the safety outcome from the 6-month RCT, which reported no serious adverse events associated with the TRE intervention in adults with obesity and type 2 diabetes.

question: What evidence supports the use of re-challenge with immune checkpoint inhibitors after prior immune-related adverse events, and how do recurrence rates of toxicity compare with antitumor efficacy upon re-challenge?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Recurrence rate of same irAE upon rechallenge
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite the specific recurrence rate (e.g., ~29%) for the same irAE upon rechallenge, as reported by Dolladille et al. (2020) from the VigiBase analysis.

content_rubrics.title: Variation in recurrence risk by organ system
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify which organ-specific irAEs (e.g., colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis) are associated with a higher recurrence risk upon rechallenge, as identified in the Dolladille et al. (2020) study.

content_rubrics.title: Incidence of any grade ≥2 irAE upon rechallenge
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the reported incidence (e.g., 38.9%) of recurrent grade ≥2 irAEs following rechallenge from the French national pharmacovigilance cohort study (Allouchery et al., 2020).

content_rubrics.title: Pattern of recurrent toxicity (same vs. different)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should detail the proportion of recurrent irAEs that are the same as the initial event versus a different irAE, as reported in the Allouchery et al. (2020) study.

content_rubrics.title: Pooled irAE incidence rates from meta-analysis
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the pooled all-grade and high-grade irAE incidence rates (e.g., 34.2% and 11.7%) after rechallenge from the systematic review/meta-analysis by Zhao et al. (2021).

content_rubrics.title: Antitumor efficacy preservation upon rechallenge
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state whether antitumor efficacy (e.g., ORR/DCR) upon rechallenge was found to be preserved or not significantly different from initial therapy, based on the systematic review/meta-analysis evidence.

content_rubrics.title: Nature of the evidence base (retrospective, no RCTs)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should correctly characterize the predominant evidence as retrospective (cohorts, series, meta-analyses) and note the absence of large randomized trials specifically on rechallenge.

question: In sickle cell disease, how do clinical outcomes and adverse event profiles compare between autologous hematopoietic stem cell gene addition therapy (lentiviral anti-sickling globin) and CRISPR-based BCL11A enhancer editing?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: VOC elimination efficacy comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should compare the reported efficacy in eliminating severe vaso-occlusive crises (VOCs) for each therapy. For CRISPR-based BCL11A editing (exa-cel), it should mention the 97% (29/30 patients) rate of freedom from severe VOCs for ≥12 consecutive months. For lentiviral gene addition (LentiGlobin), it should mention the reported complete resolution of severe vaso-occlusive events in the pivotal Group C analysis.

content_rubrics.title: Hospitalization reduction comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should compare the impact on VOC-related hospitalizations. For exa-cel, it should specify that 100% (30/30) of evaluable patients were free from hospitalizations for VOCs for at least 12 consecutive months. For LentiGlobin, the answer should address the reported resolution of severe events leading to hospitalization.

content_rubrics.title: Engraftment and neutrophil recovery
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that successful neutrophil and platelet engraftment occurred in all patients treated with exa-cel (44/44). While the abstract for LentiGlobin does not explicitly state engraftment rates, a complete answer would note the successful autologous transplantation process as a common outcome for both therapies.

content_rubrics.title: Common safety profile anchor
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the safety profile for both therapies is largely anchored to the myeloablative conditioning regimen (busulfan) and the process of autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation, as explicitly noted for exa-cel.

content_rubrics.title: Absence of treatment-related cancers
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explicitly mention that no cancers occurred in the exa-cel study cohort (with median 19.3 months follow-up), which is a critical safety endpoint for gene therapies. This point should be contrasted with the theoretical long-term risks associated with lentiviral vector integration.

content_rubrics.title: Follow-up duration context
content_rubrics.description: The answer should provide context on the available follow-up time, such as the median follow-up of 19.3 months (range, 0.8 to 48.1) for the exa-cel study, which is necessary for interpreting the durability and safety of the response.

content_rubrics.title: SCFA reduction and link to inflammation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that PD patients show reduced fecal short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and that these microbial metabolites are linked to intestinal inflammation markers like calprotectin, as found in the study by Aho et al. (PMID: 33557896).

content_rubrics.title: SCFAs modulate alpha-synuclein aggregation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that SCFAs (acetate, propionate, butyrate) produced by the gut microbiome can modulate alpha-synuclein aggregation, a key pathological hallmark of PD, as discussed in the review (PMID: 38377788).

content_rubrics.title: Gut inflammation precedes systemic inflammation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that gut inflammatory signals (e.g., elevated fecal calprotectin) in PD are not well mirrored by plasma inflammation markers, suggesting a primary intestinal inflammatory process, as indicated by the findings from Aho et al. (PMID: 33557896).

content_rubrics.title: Microbiome alterations and neuroinflammation link
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that alterations in the gut microbiome contribute to PD pathogenesis through mechanisms involving neuroinflammation and oxidative stress, as outlined in the review (PMID: 36332796).

content_rubrics.title: Dietary influence on microbiome and PD risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the proposed neurodetrimental effects of a Western diet and neuroprotective effects of Mediterranean diets on the gut-brain axis in PD, as suggested for further exploration in human trials (PMID: 36332796).

question: How do integrase strand transfer inhibitor resistance mutations affect viral fitness and cross-resistance patterns across different integrase inhibitors in HIV-1?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Fitness cost of primary EVG resistance mutations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that primary elvitegravir (EVG) resistance-associated mutations (T66I/A/K, E92Q/G, T97A, S147G, Q148R/H/K, N155H) reduce viral replication fitness and integrase enzyme function, as demonstrated by Abram et al. (2013).

content_rubrics.title: Impact of dual primary mutations on fitness and resistance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that dual combinations of primary INSTI mutations further reduce both drug susceptibility and viral replication fitness relative to single mutations, which may limit their in vivo co-occurrence, as shown by Abram et al. (2013).

content_rubrics.title: Cross-resistance patterns for first-generation INSTIs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that certain primary EVG mutations (e.g., T66K, E92Q, Q148R/H/K, N155H) confer broad cross-resistance to raltegravir (RAL), while others (e.g., T66I/A, E92G, T97A, S147G) retain susceptibility to RAL, based on findings from Abram et al. (2013).

content_rubrics.title: DTG susceptibility with single vs. dual primary mutations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that susceptibility to dolutegravir (DTG) is retained with single primary INSTI mutations but can be reduced by dual mutation combinations involving Q148R, according to Abram et al. (2013).

content_rubrics.title: Fitness effect of R263K + M50I combination
content_rubrics.description: The answer should address that the combination of the R263K resistance mutation with the polymorphic M50I substitution increases drug resistance but does not restore viral replicative fitness, as indicated by the study on HIV-1 subtype B integrase (PMID: 24433497).

content_rubrics.description: The model should accurately define the primary endpoint of 'transfusion independence' as used in the clinical trials: a weighted average hemoglobin level of ≥9 g/dL without red-cell transfusions for a sustained period (≥12 months).

content_rubrics.title: AAV vs LNP hepatocyte editing efficiency comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should compare the reported hepatocyte editing efficiency percentages achieved by AAV-delivered editors versus lipid nanoparticle (LNP)-delivered editors in relevant in vivo studies.

content_rubrics.title: Off-target profile differences between delivery platforms
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe and contrast the off-target editing profiles (e.g., frequency, types of off-target sites) associated with AAV-mediated delivery versus LNP-mediated delivery of gene editors.

content_rubrics.title: Innate immune activation by AAV capsids
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention specific innate immune responses triggered by AAV vectors, such as Toll-like receptor (TLR) activation, cytokine induction, or neutralizing antibody formation, relevant to liver-directed delivery.

content_rubrics.title: Innate immune activation by LNP components
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention specific innate immune responses triggered by LNP delivery, such as activation by ionizable lipids or other components, leading to cytokine release or other inflammatory markers.

content_rubrics.title: Persistence of editing effect comparison
content_rubrics.description: The answer should compare the duration and stability of gene editing in hepatocytes following AAV delivery (often leading to long-term transgene expression) versus LNP delivery (typically transient expression).

content_rubrics.title: Dose-dependent immune responses
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss how innate immune activation for both AAV and LNP delivery methods can be dose-dependent and how this impacts the therapeutic window and safety profile.

content_rubrics.title: Pre-existing immunity impact on AAV efficacy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should address the issue of pre-existing neutralizing antibodies against AAV serotypes and how this affects editing efficiency and immune activation compared to LNP delivery.

content_rubrics.title: Editor cargo and immune activation link
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain how the gene editor cargo itself (e.g., Cas9 mRNA, gRNA) can contribute to innate immune activation differently when delivered via AAV versus LNP.

question: In multiple sclerosis, what evidence supports Epstein-Barr virus exposure or reactivation as a causal or contributing factor in disease onset and progression?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Longitudinal evidence of EBV preceding MS onset
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the specific finding from the large US military cohort (Bjornevik et al., 2022) that MS risk increased dramatically (32-fold) only after EBV seroconversion, establishing a temporal sequence and a large effect size.

content_rubrics.title: Association between infectious mononucleosis and MS risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should discuss the established epidemiological link, supported by meta-analyses, that a history of symptomatic primary EBV infection (infectious mononucleosis) is associated with an approximately 2-fold higher risk of developing MS.

content_rubrics.title: Molecular mimicry mechanism: EBNA1 and GlialCAM
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the key mechanistic finding (Lanz et al., 2022) that clonally expanded B cells in MS patients can produce antibodies that cross-react with both the EBV protein EBNA1 and the CNS protein GlialCAM, providing a direct link between EBV immunity and CNS autoimmunity.

content_rubrics.title: EBV as a potential necessary cause for MS
content_rubrics.description: Based on the longitudinal data, the answer should reference the strong causal argument presented in the retrieved literature (Bjornevik et al., 2022), which suggests EBV may be the leading or a necessary cause of MS, as MS risk was negligible prior to EBV infection.

content_rubrics.title: Lack of shared HLA basis for IM and MS
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge the genetic complexity by mentioning the finding (Ramagopalan et al., 2011) that HLA-DRB1*01:01 is associated with infectious mononucleosis but is protective against MS, indicating the association is not explained by simple shared HLA susceptibility.

content_rubrics.title: Biomarker evidence: Neurofilament light chain increase post-EBV
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the biomarker evidence from the longitudinal study (Bjornevik et al., 2022) showing that serum levels of neurofilament light chain, a marker of neuroaxonal injury, increased only after EBV seroconversion in individuals who later developed MS.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that Clinically Isolated Syndrome (CIS) is a precursor to Multiple Sclerosis (MS) and provide a range for typical conversion rates to Clinically Definite MS (CDMS), such as the 20-75% or 38-68% figures found in the retrieved papers.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that brain MRI findings are used to identify CIS patients at high risk for conversion to CDMS, as the retrieved papers state that 50-80% of CIS patients have clinically silent brain lesions at presentation, and a high percentage of those with abnormal MRI convert to CDMS.

content_rubrics.title: Efficacy of early Interferon beta in reducing CDMS conversion risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should cite specific risk reduction percentages for early Interferon beta (IFNβ) treatment from clinical trials, such as the 44-50% reduction in the risk of progression to CDMS mentioned in the retrieved paper (PMID: 21485162).

content_rubrics.title: Long-term benefit of early Interferon beta treatment
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the long-term (e.g., 5-year) follow-up data from extension studies showing sustained benefit, such as the 35-37% reduced risk of conversion to CDMS for patients receiving early IFNβ treatment compared to delayed treatment, as stated in the retrieved paper (PMID: 21485162).

content_rubrics.title: Rationale for early DMT initiation in CIS
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain the biological rationale for early treatment, specifically that axonal damage occurs in the CIS stage and early DMT might prevent this damage to slow disability progression, as outlined in the retrieved paper (PMID: 21485162).

all_rubrics.description: The answer should cite specific risk reduction percentages for early Interferon beta (IFNβ) treatment from clinical trials, such as the 44-50% reduction in the risk of progression to CDMS mentioned in the retrieved paper (PMID: 21485162).

all_rubrics.title: Long-term benefit of early Interferon beta treatment
all_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the long-term (e.g., 5-year) follow-up data from extension studies showing sustained benefit, such as the 35-37% reduced risk of conversion to CDMS for patients receiving early IFNβ treatment compared to delayed treatment, as stated in the retrieved paper (PMID: 21485162).

all_rubrics.title: Rationale for early DMT initiation in CIS
all_rubrics.description: The answer should explain the biological rationale for early treatment, specifically that axonal damage occurs in the CIS stage and early DMT might prevent this damage to slow disability progression, as outlined in the retrieved paper (PMID: 21485162).

question: In EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, what molecular mechanisms most commonly drive acquired resistance to third-generation EGFR inhibitors, and how frequently are they observed across clinical cohorts?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: EGFR C797X as primary on-target resistance mechanism
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that secondary EGFR kinase domain mutations, specifically C797S/C797X substitutions, are a common on-target resistance mechanism that disrupts covalent binding of osimertinib.

content_rubrics.title: Other recurrent EGFR on-target mutations (L718, L792, G796)
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include that beyond C797X, other recurrent EGFR substitutions like L718, L792, and G796 can also confer resistance to third-generation EGFR inhibitors.

content_rubrics.title: MET amplification as a bypass resistance pathway
content_rubrics.description: The answer must state that activation of bypass pathways, particularly MET amplification, is a common EGFR-independent resistance mechanism observed in clinical cohorts.

content_rubrics.title: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) transformation as resistance
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention histologic transformation, specifically to small cell lung cancer (SCLC), as a phenotypic resistance mechanism identified in patients progressing on EGFR TKIs.

content_rubrics.title: Concurrent resistance mechanisms in patients with low T790M RAF
content_rubrics.description: Based on PMID 33209615, the answer should note that patients with a plasma T790M relative allele frequency (RAF) less than 20% are more likely to harbor concurrent resistance mechanisms (like MET amplification or SCLC transformation).

content_rubrics.title: Impact of resistance mechanisms on osimertinib response rates
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference that the presence of certain concurrent resistance mechanisms (identified via low T790M RAF) correlates with significantly lower objective response rates (ORR) and disease control rates (DCR) to osimertinib.

question: In patients with stable coronary artery disease, how does adding low-dose rivaroxaban to aspirin compare with aspirin alone for preventing major adverse cardiovascular events and increasing major bleeding risk?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Primary outcome results from COMPASS trial
content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the primary composite outcome (cardiovascular death, stroke, or myocardial infarction) rates for rivaroxaban 2.5 mg twice daily plus aspirin versus aspirin alone: 4.1% vs 5.4% with a hazard ratio of 0.76 (95% CI 0.66–0.86).

content_rubrics.title: Major bleeding risk increase
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the combination increased major bleeding events versus aspirin alone: 3.1% vs 1.9% with a hazard ratio of 1.70 (95% CI 1.40–2.05).

content_rubrics.title: Lack of significant increase in fatal bleeding
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that the COMPASS trial found no significant difference in intracranial or fatal bleeding between the rivaroxaban-plus-aspirin group and the aspirin-alone group.

content_rubrics.title: All-cause mortality reduction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should include the all-cause mortality data from COMPASS: 3.4% in the rivaroxaban-plus-aspirin group vs 4.1% in the aspirin-alone group (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.71–0.96).

content_rubrics.title: Applicability to stable CAD subgroup
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the prespecified COMPASS subgroup analysis of patients with chronic coronary syndromes, particularly those with prior PCI, showing consistent benefit (MACE HR 0.74) and harm (major bleeding HR 1.72).

content_rubrics.title: Comparison to rivaroxaban monotherapy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that in COMPASS, rivaroxaban 5 mg twice daily alone did not result in significantly better cardiovascular outcomes than aspirin alone, but did increase major bleeding.

question: What molecular mechanisms drive p53 pathway activation and clonal selection after CRISPR-Cas9 editing in human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: p38 MAPK-ROS axis drives proliferation stress and DNA damage
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that ex vivo activation of HSPCs triggers p38 MAPK phosphorylation, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS), which promotes rapid cell-cycle progression and proliferation-induced DNA damage, exacerbating the p53 response beyond the initial DSBs.

content_rubrics.title: Ex vivo culture duration as a key determinant of p53 response
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that shortening ex vivo culture time mitigates the p53-mediated DNA damage response to CRISPR-Cas9-induced DSBs, enhancing the reconstitution capacity of edited HSPCs, but at the cost of lower HDR efficiency.

content_rubrics.title: p53 stabilization via canonical DDR from DSBs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the mechanism where Cas9-induced double-strand breaks activate upstream DNA damage sensors (ATM/ATR) and transducers (CHK1/CHK2), leading to p53 stabilization and the induction of its transcriptional targets for cell-cycle arrest, apoptosis, or senescence.

content_rubrics.title: Proliferation stress as a driver of HSPC dysfunction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should identify proliferative stress, induced by ex vivo culture and activation, as a fundamental driver of HSPC dysfunction that shapes clonal outcomes, with implications for designing safer gene correction strategies.

content_rubrics.title: p38 inhibition improves HSC fitness and polyclonality
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that p38 inhibition before gene editing delays the G1/S transition, expands transcriptionally defined HSCs, and endows edited cells with superior multi-lineage differentiation, persistence, a more polyclonal repertoire, and better-preserved genome integrity.

content_rubrics.title: Clonal selection shaped by p53-dependent fitness differences
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that the p53-dependent checkpoints create strong fitness differences among edited cells, which then determines which clones successfully contribute to long-term engraftment, a process referred to as clonal selection.

question: In methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) bacteremia, does early switch from vancomycin to daptomycin improve clinical outcomes compared with continued vancomycin therapy?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Overall mortality comparison from meta-analysis
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the 2024 meta-analysis of 20 studies found daptomycin treatment was associated with a non-significant trend toward lower mortality compared to vancomycin (OR = 0.81; 95% CI, 0.62, 1.06).

content_rubrics.title: Benefit of early switch within 3 or 5 days
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that sub-analyses in the 2024 meta-analysis found switching to daptomycin within 3 or 5 days was significantly associated with 55% and 45% decreased odds of all-cause mortality, respectively.

content_rubrics.title: Lack of benefit from late switch
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that switching to daptomycin any time after five days of treatment was not significantly associated with lower odds of mortality, according to the 2024 meta-analysis.

content_rubrics.title: Large cohort finding on 3-day switch
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite the large VA cohort (n=7411) finding that switching from vancomycin to daptomycin within 3 days was associated with lower 30-day mortality (adjusted HR 0.48; 95% CI 0.25–0.92).

content_rubrics.title: No benefit from later switch in large cohort
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that in the same large VA cohort, switching at any time later during hospitalization was not significantly associated with lower mortality (HR 0.87; 95% CI 0.69–1.09).

content_rubrics.title: Benefit in high vancomycin MIC subgroup
content_rubrics.description: The model should note the stratified analysis from the 2024 meta-analysis showing daptomycin treatment among patients infected with MRSA strains with vancomycin MIC≥1 mg/L was significantly associated with 40% lower odds of mortality.

content_rubrics.title: Outcome of early switch in elevated MIC cohort
content_rubrics.description: The model should reference the matched cohort study focused on elevated vancomycin MIC (>1 µg/mL) where patients switched early (<72 hours) had lower clinical failure (30-day mortality or persistent bacteremia ≥7 days) versus continued vancomycin (20.0% vs 48.2%).

content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that in the international multicenter retrospective cohort study (Meinel TR et al., 2023), recent DOAC ingestion (≤48h) was associated with lower adjusted odds of symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage (sICH) compared to no anticoagulation (adjusted OR 0.57, 95% CI 0.36–0.92).

content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that the systematic review and meta-analysis (Ghannam M et al., 2023) found no significant difference in sICH rates between DOAC and non-DOAC groups (3.4% vs 3.5%; pooled OR 0.95, 95% CI 0.67–1.36).

content_rubrics.title: Overall sICH rate in DOAC patients from meta-analysis
content_rubrics.description: The model should cite the pooled sICH rate specifically for the DOAC group from the meta-analysis, which was 3% (95% CI, 3-4).

content_rubrics.title: Functional outcome comparison from meta-analysis
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that the meta-analysis reported no significant difference in 90-day functional independence (modified Rankin scale score 0-2) between DOAC and non-DOAC groups (46.4% vs 56.8%; OR, 1.21 [95% CI, 0.400-3.67]).

content_rubrics.title: Evidence on idarucizumab use
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that the meta-analysis found no difference in sICH rate based on the administration of idarucizumab.

content_rubrics.title: Nature of the evidence base
content_rubrics.description: The model should explicitly characterize the evidence as observational and from registries (no randomized trials), as stated in the retrieved papers, and note that further prospective randomized trials are warranted.

content_rubrics.title: Tolebrutinib efficacy on 6-month confirmed disability progression
content_rubrics.description: The answer must report the key efficacy outcome from the HERCULES trial: the percentage of participants with confirmed disability progression sustained for at least 6 months in the tolebrutinib group (22.6%) versus the placebo group (30.7%), along with the hazard ratio (0.69) and its statistical significance (P=0.003). This data is directly from the primary endpoint results of the retrieved paper (PMID: 40202696).

content_rubrics.title: Study population and design for progressive MS data
content_rubrics.description: The answer must specify that the primary efficacy data for progressive MS comes from a specific phase 3, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (HERCULES) involving participants with nonrelapsing secondary progressive MS, as described in the retrieved paper (PMID: 40202696). It should correctly identify the trial name and patient population.

content_rubrics.title: Absence of MRI data on chronic active lesions in progressive MS
content_rubrics.description: The answer must explicitly state that the provided abstract for the key progressive MS trial (PMID: 40202696) does not report quantitative MRI outcomes specifically for chronic active lesions (e.g., slowly expanding lesions, paramagnetic rim lesions). This is a verifiable statement based on the content of the retrieved abstract.

content_rubrics.title: Distinction between MRI outcomes in relapsing vs. progressive MS
content_rubrics.description: The answer must correctly differentiate that while MRI efficacy (e.g., reduction in new gadolinium-enhancing lesions) has been shown for tolebrutinib in relapsing MS (as indicated in the abstract for PMID: 34418400), this evidence does not directly answer the question about MRI measures of chronic active lesions in progressive MS.

content_rubrics.title: Mechanism of action relevance to progressive MS
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the proposed mechanism by which BTK inhibitors like tolebrutinib might impact progressive MS, specifically their action on myeloid cells and microglia within the CNS, which are implicated in chronic neuroinflammation linked to disability accrual. This context is provided in the background section of the retrieved paper (PMID: 40202696).

content_rubrics.description: The answer should include key safety findings from the HERCULES trial, specifically the higher incidence of serious adverse events (15.0% vs. 10.4%) and increases in alanine aminotransferase levels (>3x ULN in 4.0% vs. 1.6%) in the tolebrutinib group compared to placebo, as reported in the results/conclusions of the retrieved paper (PMID: 40202696).

content_rubrics.description: The answer should report the specific 24-hour electrographic seizure freedom rates from the NEOLEV2 RCT: 80% (24/30) for phenobarbital versus 28% (15/53) for levetiracetam, with a statistically significant P-value (< .001).

content_rubrics.title: Levetiracetam Dose Escalation Effect
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that increasing the levetiracetam dose from 40 to 60 mg/kg in the NEOLEV2 trial resulted in only a small absolute efficacy improvement (7.5%).

content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that the 2023 Cochrane review concluded phenobarbital is probably more effective than levetiracetam for seizure control after loading doses, based on moderate-certainty evidence.

content_rubrics.description: The answer should note the trend from the NEOLEV2 trial where more adverse effects were seen in the phenobarbital group compared to the levetiracetam group, while also clarifying that this difference was not statistically significant and the trial was not powered for safety outcomes.

content_rubrics.title: ILAE Guideline First-Line Recommendation
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify that the 2023 ILAE Task Force guidelines recommend phenobarbital as the first-line ASM for neonatal seizures, based on higher certainty of evidence for its efficacy compared to levetiracetam.

content_rubrics.title: ILAE Guideline on Levetiracetam Use
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention the ILAE Task Force's conditional recommendation regarding levetiracetam: it may be considered as an alternative first-line treatment when there are concerns about phenobarbital's adverse effects, despite lower certainty of evidence for its efficacy.

question: In patients with acute myocardial infarction and multivessel coronary artery disease, does complete revascularization compared with culprit-only PCI reduce long-term mortality and recurrent myocardial infarction?

content_rubrics.title: COMPLETE trial's primary outcome result
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that in the COMPLETE trial, complete revascularization reduced the composite of cardiovascular death or myocardial infarction versus culprit-only PCI (HR 0.74) at a median follow-up of approximately 3 years.

content_rubrics.title: FULL REVASC trial's key secondary outcome result
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that the FULL REVASC trial found no significant reduction in death or myocardial infarction (the key secondary outcome) with FFR-guided complete revascularization compared to culprit-only PCI at a median follow-up of 4.8 years.

content_rubrics.title: DANAMI-3—PRIMULTI trial's endpoint driver
content_rubrics.description: The model should explain that in the DANAMI-3—PRIMULTI trial, the benefit of complete FFR-guided revascularization on the composite endpoint was primarily driven by a reduction in repeat revascularizations, not by a reduction in mortality or recurrent myocardial infarction.

content_rubrics.title: OPTION-STEMI trial's primary endpoint comparison
content_rubrics.description: The model should reference the OPTION-STEMI trial's finding that immediate complete revascularization was not non-inferior to staged complete revascularization for the composite endpoint of death, MI, or unplanned revascularization at 1 year (HR 1.24, 95% CI 0.86-1.79; p for non-inferiority = 0.24).

content_rubrics.title: In-hospital complication rate in OPTION-STEMI
content_rubrics.description: The model should note the OPTION-STEMI trial's observation of a higher rate of cardiogenic shock during the index hospitalization in the immediate complete revascularization group (4%) compared to the staged group (2%).

content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that in recent trials like FULL REVASC and DANAMI-3—PRIMULTI, the complete revascularization strategy was guided by fractional flow reserve (FFR) for non-culprit lesion assessment.

question: What is the association between long-term antiseizure medication use and bone health outcomes (osteopenia, osteoporosis, fractures) in adults with epilepsy?

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage
tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

tool_rubrics.title: Cite correct PMIDs
tool_rubrics.description: Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

tool_rubrics.title: Provide year and journal info
tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Enzyme-inducing ASMs and fracture risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (EIAEDs) are associated with an increased risk of fractures compared to non-EIAEDs, based on systematic review evidence. This includes specific hazard ratio increases (e.g., 9-22% for any fracture, 49-53% for hip fracture) as reported in the largest and most methodologically rigorous study (PMID: 26354168).

content_rubrics.title: Mixed evidence on bone mineral density
content_rubrics.description: The answer should acknowledge that the literature presents conflicting results regarding the effect of enzyme-inducing ASMs on bone mineral density (BMD). It should state that systematic reviews show studies are split, with some finding decreased BMD in EIAED users and others finding no effect (PMID: 26354168).

content_rubrics.title: Specific high-risk medications
content_rubrics.description: The answer should specify which antiseizure medications have the clearest association with adverse bone outcomes. It must name phenytoin, phenobarbital, and primidone as enzyme-inducing AEDs with the clearest link to decreased BMD, and note that carbamazepine and valproate may also adversely affect bone (PMID: 15123008).

content_rubrics.title: Association duration and drug load
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that lower bone mineral density is frequently associated with longer duration of therapy and higher 'drug load' (mono- vs polytherapy), as demonstrated in cohort studies (PMID: 18467202).

content_rubrics.title: Fracture risk multifactorial nature
content_rubrics.description: The answer should explain that the increased fracture risk in people with epilepsy is multifactorial, related both to the effects of antiseizure medications and to seizure-related trauma or falls (PMID: 15123008).

content_rubrics.title: Evidence base limitations
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the evidence for the association is largely observational, that studies are significantly heterogeneous, and that more large, methodologically rigorous studies are needed to confirm the findings (PMID: 26354168).

question: In HIV therapy, how does the use of proton pump inhibitors impact virologic suppression rates in patients taking oral antiretrovirals with pH-dependent absorption, and what mitigation strategies are supported by clinical evidence?
topic: Drug–drug interaction affecting efficacy / Antiretroviral pharmacology

tool_rubrics.title: Correct pubmed_search usage

tool_rubrics.description: Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name

content_rubrics.title: Atazanavir exposure reduction with PPIs
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that co-administration of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) can reduce plasma concentrations of atazanavir, as explicitly noted in the abstract of PMID 18507524.

content_rubrics.title: Ritonavir boosting to overcome PPI interaction
content_rubrics.description: The answer should mention that ritonavir boosting is a strategy used to overcome the drug interaction between PPIs and atazanavir, as indicated in the abstract of PMID 18507524 ('Boosting ATV exposure with ritonavir (r) may make it possible to overcome these drug interactions').

content_rubrics.title: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM) as a mitigation strategy
content_rubrics.description: The answer should describe the use of Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM) as a strategy to guide therapy changes (e.g., switching from boosted to unboosted atazanavir) and identify patients at risk of suboptimal drug exposure, as detailed in the study methodology and results of PMID 18507524.

content_rubrics.title: Clinical consequence: Risk of virologic failure
content_rubrics.description: The answer should reference the specific clinical outcome from PMID 18507524 where, after switching from boosted to unboosted atazanavir guided by TDM, one patient experienced virological failure, illustrating the real risk of suppressed efficacy.

content_rubrics.title: PPIs as contraindication for rilpivirine
content_rubrics.description: The answer should state that proton pump inhibitors are contraindicated with rilpivirine, as this is a critical clinical point derived from the management guidelines discussed in PMID 24068877.

content_rubrics.title: Tenofovir's role in compounding interaction risk
content_rubrics.description: The answer should note that the presence of tenofovir (TDF) was a common factor in patients who fell below the target atazanavir trough concentration after switching regimens, as reported in PMID 18507524 ('TDF was part of the antiretroviral regimen in all four cases').

content_rubrics.title: SCOT-HEART: LAP burden strongest MI predictor
content_rubrics.description: The model should state that in the SCOT-HEART trial analysis (PMID 32174130), low-attenuation plaque (LAP) burden was the strongest predictor of fatal or nonfatal myocardial infarction, with an adjusted hazard ratio of 1.60 per doubling of LAP burden.

content_rubrics.title: LAP prognostic value independent of stenosis
content_rubrics.description: The model should specify that the prognostic value of LAP burden for future MI was independent of and held irrespective of coronary artery area stenosis severity, as reported in the SCOT-HEART analysis (PMID 32174130).

content_rubrics.title: Specific LAP burden threshold for high risk
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention the specific quantitative threshold from SCOT-HEART (PMID 32174130): patients with an LAP burden greater than 4% had a nearly 5-fold higher risk of subsequent MI (HR 4.65).

content_rubrics.title: LAP correlation with stenosis severity
content_rubrics.description: The model should note that while LAP burden carries independent prognostic value, it correlates very strongly with luminal stenosis severity (r=0.83 for area stenosis), as found in the SCOT-HEART study (PMID 32174130).

question: In Duchenne muscular dystrophy, what evidence supports exon skipping via genome editing versus micro-dystrophin gene transfer in improving muscle function, and how do their safety profiles differ?
topic: Gene therapy strategies for muscular dystrophy

tool_rubrics.description: Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format

content_rubrics.title: Next-generation exon skipping AON efficacy improvements
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that next-generation antisense oligonucleotides (AONs) with 2'-O-methyl-modified phosphorothioate RNA chemistry targeting alternative binding sites achieved 65-100 times higher exon skipping levels compared to first-generation drugs, restoring dystrophin up to 40% of healthy control and improving motor function in preclinical models.

content_rubrics.title: Exon skipping safety profile observations
content_rubrics.description: The model should discuss safety findings from preclinical studies, specifically noting that next-generation AONs showed no major safety observations and demonstrated improved therapeutic index in preclinical mouse models.

content_rubrics.title: CRISPR-guided exon skipping functional outcomes
content_rubrics.description: The model should describe that CRISPR-guided exon skipping in DMD models rescued dystrophin deficiency, with therapeutic exon skipping through CRISPR-guided cytidine deaminase restoring dystrophin and cardiac function in vivo preclinical models.

content_rubrics.title: Exon 51 skipping AON regulatory status
content_rubrics.description: The model should mention that first-generation exon 51 skipping AONs have reached conditional regulatory US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval for DMD, though with limited efficacy that prompted development of next-generation improvements.

content_rubrics.title: Micro-dystrophin gene transfer evidence
content_rubrics.description: The model should address evidence supporting micro-dystrophin gene transfer approaches, including any preclinical functional outcomes, safety profiles, or comparative efficacy data relative to exon skipping methods from the retrieved papers.

content_rubrics.title: Comparative safety profiles between methods
content_rubrics.description: The model should provide specific safety comparisons between exon skipping via genome editing and micro-dystrophin gene transfer approaches, including any therapeutic indices, safety observations, or risk profiles mentioned in the retrieved papers.

content_rubrics.title: Preclinical model validation
content_rubrics.description: The model should reference the specific preclinical models used in exon skipping studies, including hDMD, hDMDdel52/mdx mice, and Dmd E4* murine models, and how these validated functional improvements.

content_rubrics.title: Dystrophin restoration quantification
content_rubrics.description: The model should include quantitative data on dystrophin restoration levels achieved through different exon skipping methods, such as percentages of healthy control dystrophin levels and correlation with functional improvements.
